Guidelines for Questions:
- Available Difficulties: Easy, Intermediate, Hard
- Available Topics: Collections, Multithreading, Core Java, JVM, OOP, Exceptions, Streams
- Make sure each question has an id, subject, topic, difficulty, title, question, answer, explanation, code_example (optional), and key_takeaways (array).

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: == vs .equals() in Java
questions.question: What is the difference between == and .equals() in Java?
questions.answer: == is the reference equality operator — it checks whether two variables point to the exact same object in memory. .equals() is a method defined in the Object class that checks logical equality — whether the contents or values of two objects are the same. For primitive types (int, char, boolean, etc.), == compares values directly since primitives are not objects. For String and other objects, == checks if both references point to the same memory location, while .equals() checks if the actual content/data is the same. It's important to note that the String class (and most Java standard library classes) override .equals() to compare content. If you define a custom class and don't override .equals(), it falls back to Object's default, which behaves like ==.
questions.explanation: Think of == as checking whether two people are literally the same person (same ID card), while .equals() checks whether two people have the same name. Two different people can have the same name (.equals() returns true), but they are not the same person (== returns false). Always use .equals() to compare object content — especially Strings.
key_takeaways: == compares memory references (address), not content
.equals() compares logical content/value of objects
For primitives, == always compares values — no .equals() available
String literals may share the same pool object, making == accidentally true — always use .equals() for safety
Custom classes should override .equals() (and hashCode()) for meaningful comparison
Calling .equals() on a null reference throws NullPointerException — use Objects.equals() for null-safe comparison

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: final, finally, and finalize
questions.question: What is the difference between final, finally, and finalize in Java?
questions.answer: These three look similar but serve completely different purposes. (1) final is a keyword used as a modifier: when applied to a variable, it makes it a constant (cannot be reassigned); when applied to a method, it prevents overriding in subclasses; when applied to a class, it prevents inheritance. (2) finally is a block used with try-catch for exception handling. Code inside the finally block always executes whether or not an exception is thrown — it is typically used for cleanup actions like closing files or database connections. (3) finalize() is a method in the Object class that the Garbage Collector calls just before an object is removed from memory. It was used for cleanup of native resources but is deprecated since Java 9 and should not be used in modern code — use try-with-resources or explicit close() instead.
questions.explanation: A simple memory trick: final = 'this cannot change', finally = 'this always runs', finalize = 'goodbye, I'm being deleted'. They share the same prefix but operate in completely different domains — one is about modification, one is about flow control, and one is about memory management.
key_takeaways: final is a modifier: makes variables constant, prevents method overriding, prevents class inheritance
finally is a try-catch block that always executes, used for cleanup logic
finalize() is an Object method called by GC before object deletion — deprecated since Java 9
A finally block runs even if a return statement is in the try block
finally does NOT run if System.exit() is called or the JVM crashes
Prefer try-with-resources over finalize() for resource cleanup in modern Java

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer
questions.question: What are the differences between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer in Java?
questions.answer: String is immutable — once created, its content cannot be changed. Every modification creates a new String object in memory, which can be expensive in loops. StringBuilder is mutable — its content can be modified without creating new objects. It is not thread-safe (no synchronization), making it faster and the preferred choice for single-threaded scenarios. StringBuffer is also mutable but is thread-safe because its methods are synchronized. This makes it slightly slower than StringBuilder due to synchronization overhead. In summary: use String for constant values, StringBuilder for string manipulation in single-threaded code, and StringBuffer only when multiple threads may modify the same buffer.
questions.explanation: Imagine String as a stone tablet — to change what's written, you have to chisel a whole new one. StringBuilder is like a whiteboard — you can erase and rewrite quickly. StringBuffer is like a whiteboard with a lock — only one person can write at a time, which makes it safe for multiple writers but slower.
key_takeaways: String is immutable — any modification creates a new object
StringBuilder is mutable and not thread-safe — fastest for single-threaded string manipulation
StringBuffer is mutable and thread-safe (synchronized) — use only when needed in multithreaded context
Concatenating strings in a loop using + is very inefficient — always use StringBuilder in loops
All three classes reside in java.lang and don't need explicit import
String literals are stored in the String Pool (part of Heap); new String() creates a separate object

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Method Overloading vs Method Overriding
questions.question: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding in Java?
questions.answer: Method Overloading (Compile-time Polymorphism): Defining multiple methods in the same class with the same name but different parameter lists (different number, type, or order of parameters). The return type alone cannot distinguish overloaded methods. The correct method to call is determined at compile time based on the method signature. Method Overriding (Runtime Polymorphism): Providing a new implementation of a method in a subclass that has the same name, return type, and parameter list as a method in the parent class. The @Override annotation is recommended to catch errors. The method to call is determined at runtime based on the actual object type. Overriding requires an inheritance relationship and cannot be done with static, final, or private methods.
questions.explanation: Overloading is like a printer that has one button called 'print' — you can print a single page, multiple pages, or in color, all with the same button name but different inputs. Overriding is like a child doing homework differently than how the teacher demonstrated — the task is the same, but the child's approach replaces the original for that specific instance.
key_takeaways: Overloading: same class, same name, different parameters — resolved at compile time
Overriding: parent-child classes, same signature — resolved at runtime
Overloading is compile-time (static) polymorphism; overriding is runtime (dynamic) polymorphism
Return type alone is not enough to distinguish overloaded methods
Use @Override annotation to get compile-time safety when overriding
static, final, and private methods cannot be overridden
Constructors can be overloaded but cannot be overridden

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions
questions.question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
questions.answer: Checked Exceptions are exceptions that the compiler forces you to handle or declare. They extend Exception (but not RuntimeException). If a method can throw a checked exception, it must either use a try-catch block to handle it or declare it with the throws keyword in the method signature. Examples: IOException, SQLException, FileNotFoundException. Unchecked Exceptions (also called Runtime Exceptions) extend RuntimeException. The compiler does NOT require you to handle them. They usually represent programming errors like null pointer access, array index out of bounds, or illegal arguments. Examples: NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, IllegalArgumentException, ClassCastException. Errors (like OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError) are a third category — they represent serious JVM-level problems that should not be caught.
questions.explanation: Checked exceptions are like filing your taxes — the government (compiler) requires you to deal with them or at least acknowledge them (declare them). Unchecked exceptions are like tripping over a rock — no one warned you in advance; they happen because of bugs or bad conditions in the code. You should write code that avoids them rather than just catching them.
key_takeaways: Checked exceptions extend Exception and must be handled or declared — enforced by the compiler
Unchecked exceptions extend RuntimeException — not required to be handled
Errors extend Error — represent serious JVM failures, should not be caught
Common checked: IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException
Common unchecked: NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, IllegalArgumentException
Prefer specific exception types over catching generic Exception
Use custom checked exceptions for recoverable conditions, unchecked for programming errors

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: ArrayList vs LinkedList
questions.question: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList in Java?
questions.answer: ArrayList is backed by a dynamic array. It provides fast random access (O(1) by index) because elements are stored in contiguous memory. However, insertions and deletions in the middle are slow (O(n)) because elements need to be shifted. LinkedList is a doubly linked list. Each element (node) stores the data plus references to the previous and next nodes. It provides fast insertions and deletions at both ends (O(1)) but slow random access (O(n)) because you must traverse the list from the beginning. Memory usage: ArrayList wastes some memory due to pre-allocated capacity; LinkedList uses more memory per element due to node pointers. In practice, ArrayList is preferred for most use cases because of cache-friendly memory access and better real-world performance.
questions.explanation: ArrayList is like a row of numbered seats in a cinema — you can instantly jump to seat 50 (random access), but inserting a seat in the middle forces everyone behind it to move. LinkedList is like a treasure hunt — each clue (node) points to the next location, so adding a clue anywhere is easy, but finding clue #50 means following all previous clues from the start.
key_takeaways: ArrayList: O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete in middle — good for frequent reads
LinkedList: O(1) insert/delete at ends, O(n) random access — good for frequent insertions/deletions
ArrayList is backed by an array; LinkedList uses doubly linked nodes
LinkedList uses more memory per element due to node pointers (prev + next)
LinkedList implements Deque — can be used as a stack or queue
ArrayList is the default choice for most use cases due to better cache performance
Both implement the List interface and maintain insertion order

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: HashMap vs Hashtable
questions.question: What is the difference between HashMap and Hashtable in Java?
questions.answer: HashMap and Hashtable both implement the Map interface and store key-value pairs using hashing, but they differ in several important ways: (1) Thread Safety: Hashtable is synchronized — all its methods are thread-safe. HashMap is not synchronized and is not thread-safe by default. (2) Null Keys/Values: HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values. Hashtable does not allow null keys or null values — it throws NullPointerException. (3) Performance: Because Hashtable is synchronized, it is slower than HashMap in single-threaded environments. (4) Iteration: HashMap uses an Iterator (fail-fast). Hashtable uses Enumeration (older API). (5) Legacy: Hashtable is a legacy class from Java 1.0. HashMap was introduced in Java 1.2 as part of the Collections Framework. For thread-safe operations, prefer ConcurrentHashMap over Hashtable.
questions.explanation: HashMap is a fast, modern key-value store — great for single-threaded apps. Hashtable is its older, slower, thread-safe cousin that's mostly retired. If you need a thread-safe map today, use ConcurrentHashMap, which is like a HashMap that safely handles multiple threads without locking the entire map.
key_takeaways: HashMap is not synchronized; Hashtable is synchronized (thread-safe)
HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values; Hashtable allows neither
HashMap is faster in single-threaded scenarios due to no synchronization overhead
Hashtable is a legacy class; prefer HashMap or ConcurrentHashMap in modern code
For thread-safe maps, use ConcurrentHashMap instead of Hashtable
Both maintain key uniqueness — duplicate keys overwrite existing values
Neither HashMap nor Hashtable guarantees insertion order — use LinkedHashMap for order

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: How HashMap Works Internally
questions.question: How does HashMap work internally in Java? Explain hashing, buckets, and collision handling.
questions.answer: HashMap internally uses an array of buckets (Node[] table) where each bucket is a linked list (or a red-black tree in Java 8+ when a bucket has more than 8 entries). When you call put(key, value): (1) The key's hashCode() method is called to compute an integer hash. (2) A secondary hash (HashMap's internal hash()) is applied to reduce clustering. (3) The bucket index is computed as (n - 1) & hash, where n is the array size. (4) If the bucket is empty, the entry is placed there. (5) If the bucket already has entries (collision), Java traverses the linked list and: overwrites if the key already exists (using equals()), or appends a new node if the key is new. When the number of entries exceeds capacity * loadFactor (default 0.75), the map is resized (rehashed) to double its capacity. Java 8 improvement: when a bucket's linked list grows beyond 8 nodes, it converts to a red-black tree for O(log n) instead of O(n) lookups.
questions.explanation: Imagine HashMap as a large parking lot divided into rows (buckets). When you park (put) a car, you take its license plate number (hashCode), apply a formula to pick a row (bucket index), and park there. If the row is full (collision), you extend it like a chain. When too many cars arrive (load factor exceeded), you expand the parking lot and re-distribute all cars. Java 8 added an optimization: if one row gets too long (8+ cars), you convert it to a tree for faster searching.
key_takeaways: HashMap uses an array of buckets internally; each bucket is a linked list (or tree in Java 8+)
hashCode() determines the bucket; equals() determines if keys match within a bucket
Collision: two keys map to the same bucket — handled via chaining (linked list)
Default initial capacity is 16; default load factor is 0.75
Resizing (rehashing) occurs when size exceeds capacity * loadFactor
Java 8+: buckets with 8+ entries convert from linked list to red-black tree for O(log n) lookup
Custom key objects must override both hashCode() and equals() for correct behavior

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: HashSet vs TreeSet vs LinkedHashSet
questions.question: What are the differences between HashSet, TreeSet, and LinkedHashSet in Java?
questions.answer: All three implement the Set interface, meaning they store unique elements only. The key differences are in ordering and performance: (1) HashSet: backed by a HashMap. No ordering guarantee — elements are stored based on hash values. Best performance: O(1) for add, remove, contains. Allows one null element. (2) TreeSet: backed by a TreeMap (red-black tree). Always maintains elements in natural sorted order (or a custom Comparator). O(log n) for all operations. Does not allow null elements. (3) LinkedHashSet: backed by a LinkedHashMap. Maintains insertion order — elements come out in the order they were added. Slightly slower than HashSet due to maintaining a linked list. Allows one null element. Use HashSet for maximum performance, TreeSet when you need sorted order, and LinkedHashSet when you need insertion order.
questions.explanation: Think of a HashSet as a bag where you throw items — no particular order, but you can quickly check if something's in there. TreeSet is like a sorted filing cabinet — everything goes in alphabetically. LinkedHashSet is like a queue — items come out in the same order you put them in.
key_takeaways: All three implement Set — no duplicate elements allowed
HashSet: O(1) operations, no order, backed by HashMap
TreeSet: O(log n) operations, always sorted, backed by TreeMap — no null allowed
LinkedHashSet: O(1) operations, insertion order preserved, backed by LinkedHashMap
TreeSet uses Comparable (natural order) or Comparator for sorting
HashSet and LinkedHashSet allow one null; TreeSet does not
Choose HashSet for speed, TreeSet for sorted data, LinkedHashSet for insertion order

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: JVM Architecture
questions.question: Explain the architecture of the JVM — what are its main components and how do they work together?
questions.answer: The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is the engine that runs compiled Java bytecode. Its main components are: (1) Class Loader Subsystem: Loads .class files into memory in three phases — Loading (reads .class file), Linking (verifies bytecode, prepares memory for static fields, resolves symbolic references), and Initialization (runs static initializers). (2) Runtime Data Areas (Memory): Method Area — stores class metadata, static variables, constants; Heap — stores all objects and instance variables; Stack — each thread has its own stack of method call frames; PC Register — holds the address of the current instruction per thread; Native Method Stack — for native (non-Java) method calls. (3) Execution Engine: Interpreter — executes bytecode line by line; JIT Compiler — compiles hot (frequently used) bytecode to native machine code for speed; Garbage Collector — automatically reclaims memory of unused objects. (4) Java Native Interface (JNI): Allows Java to call native C/C++ libraries.
questions.explanation: Think of the JVM like a kitchen. The Class Loader is the delivery service that brings in ingredients (.class files). The Runtime Data Areas are storage zones — the pantry (Method Area), the countertop (Heap), and each cook's notepad (Stack). The Execution Engine is the chef — first reading recipes slowly (Interpreter), then memorizing and executing favorite recipes super fast (JIT Compiler). The Garbage Collector is the dishwasher, cleaning up used utensils automatically.
key_takeaways: JVM has three main subsystems: Class Loader, Runtime Data Areas, and Execution Engine
Heap stores all objects and is shared across threads; Stack is per-thread and holds method frames
Method Area stores class-level data: metadata, static variables, constant pool
JIT Compiler improves performance by converting hot bytecode to native machine code
Garbage Collector automatically manages Heap memory — frees unreachable objects
Java is platform-independent because bytecode runs on the JVM, not directly on the OS
JNI allows Java to interact with native code written in C or C++

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Garbage Collection in Java
questions.question: How does Garbage Collection work in Java? Explain generations, GC algorithms, and key GC types.
questions.answer: Java's Garbage Collector (GC) automatically reclaims heap memory occupied by objects that are no longer reachable (no active references point to them). The Heap is divided into generations based on the observation that most objects die young (Generational Hypothesis): (1) Young Generation: Where all new objects are allocated. Contains Eden space and two Survivor spaces (S0, S1). Minor GC runs here frequently and is fast. (2) Old Generation (Tenured): Objects that survive multiple Minor GCs are promoted here. Major GC (or Full GC) runs here and is slower. (3) Metaspace (Java 8+): Stores class metadata (replaced PermGen from older Java). Key GC Algorithms: Serial GC — single-threaded, for small apps; Parallel GC — multi-threaded, high throughput; G1 GC (default since Java 9) — divides heap into regions, balances throughput and pause time; ZGC / Shenandoah — ultra-low pause time GCs for large heaps. Objects become eligible for GC when no live thread can reach them through any reference chain.
questions.explanation: Garbage Collection is like an automatic room cleaner. It first looks at the kids' toy area (Young Generation) — most toys used briefly are thrown away (Minor GC, fast). Toys that are kept for a while are moved to the adult storage room (Old Generation). Occasionally, the whole house is cleaned (Full GC, slower). G1 GC is like a smart cleaner who cleans the messiest rooms first rather than everything at once.
key_takeaways: GC automatically frees memory of objects with no live references — no manual memory management needed
Heap is divided into Young Generation (Eden + Survivors) and Old Generation
Minor GC: fast, cleans Young Generation; Major/Full GC: slower, cleans Old Generation
Objects are promoted from Young to Old Generation after surviving multiple Minor GCs
G1 GC is the default since Java 9 — balances throughput and pause times
System.gc() is a suggestion, not a command — JVM may ignore it
Avoid memory leaks by removing references when objects are no longer needed

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Thread Lifecycle in Java
questions.question: What are the different states in the lifecycle of a Java Thread?
questions.answer: A Java thread goes through the following states, defined in the Thread.State enum: (1) NEW: The thread has been created (new Thread()) but start() has not been called yet. (2) RUNNABLE: The thread is ready to run and may be running. After start() is called, the thread enters this state and waits for the CPU scheduler. (3) BLOCKED: The thread is waiting to acquire an object's monitor lock (waiting to enter a synchronized block). (4) WAITING: The thread is waiting indefinitely for another thread to perform a specific action — triggered by Object.wait(), Thread.join(), or LockSupport.park(). (5) TIMED_WAITING: Similar to WAITING but with a timeout — triggered by Thread.sleep(ms), Object.wait(ms), Thread.join(ms). (6) TERMINATED: The thread has finished executing its run() method or was terminated due to an exception. A thread cannot be restarted once it reaches the TERMINATED state.
questions.explanation: Think of a thread's lifecycle like a person's workday. NEW is when you've been hired but haven't shown up. RUNNABLE is when you're at your desk ready (or actually) working. BLOCKED is waiting outside the meeting room because someone else is inside. WAITING is sitting in the break room until someone calls you back. TIMED_WAITING is taking a nap and setting an alarm. TERMINATED is when you've clocked out and gone home — you can't restart the same workday.
key_takeaways: Thread states: NEW → RUNNABLE → BLOCKED/WAITING/TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED
NEW: created but start() not called; RUNNABLE: ready or running after start()
BLOCKED: waiting for a monitor lock; WAITING: waiting indefinitely for notification
TIMED_WAITING: waiting with a timeout (sleep, join with timeout, wait with timeout)
TERMINATED: run() has finished — a terminated thread cannot be restarted
Use thread.getState() to inspect the current state programmatically
Calling start() twice on the same thread throws IllegalThreadStateException

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: volatile Keyword and Memory Visibility
questions.question: What is the volatile keyword in Java and how does it solve memory visibility problems in multithreading?
questions.answer: In a multi-core system, each CPU core has its own cache. Without synchronization, one thread may update a variable in its local cache but another thread on a different core may still read a stale value from its own cache — this is a memory visibility problem. The volatile keyword tells the JVM that a variable's value must always be read from and written to main memory directly, not from CPU cache. This guarantees visibility: when one thread writes a volatile variable, all other threads immediately see the updated value. However, volatile does NOT guarantee atomicity. For example, volatile int count++; is not atomic because it involves three steps: read, increment, write. For atomic compound operations, use synchronized, AtomicInteger, or other java.util.concurrent.atomic classes. volatile is ideal for simple flag variables used to signal threads.
questions.explanation: Imagine each thread has a personal whiteboard (CPU cache) and there's one shared whiteboard on the wall (main memory). Normally, threads work from their own whiteboards and only occasionally update the shared one — leading to stale data. volatile forces every thread to always read from and write to the shared wall whiteboard directly, so everyone always sees the latest value.
key_takeaways: volatile ensures memory visibility — writes are immediately visible to all threads
Without volatile, threads may cache variable values and read stale data
volatile does NOT provide atomicity — compound operations like count++ are still not thread-safe
Use volatile for simple boolean flags or status variables shared across threads
For atomic operations, use synchronized blocks or java.util.concurrent.atomic classes
volatile also prevents instruction reordering by the JIT compiler
volatile is lighter than synchronized — no locking, no blocking

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Deadlock in Java
questions.question: What is a deadlock in Java multithreading? How does it occur and how can it be prevented?
questions.answer: A deadlock is a situation where two or more threads are permanently blocked, each waiting for a resource held by the other. It is one of the most difficult concurrency bugs. Deadlock occurs when four conditions are met simultaneously (Coffman conditions): (1) Mutual Exclusion — at least one resource is held in a non-shareable mode. (2) Hold and Wait — a thread holds one resource and waits for another. (3) No Preemption — resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread. (4) Circular Wait — thread A waits for a lock held by thread B, and thread B waits for a lock held by thread A. Prevention strategies: Always acquire locks in the same fixed order across all threads; use tryLock() with a timeout (from ReentrantLock) so threads can back off; avoid nested locks; use higher-level concurrency utilities (like java.util.concurrent) instead of raw synchronization.
questions.explanation: A deadlock is like two people in a narrow corridor, each refusing to step aside. Person A needs to get past Person B, but Person B also needs to get past Person A. Neither moves, and both are stuck forever. The key to prevention: always have everyone approach the corridor from the same side (consistent lock ordering), or use a timeout so they back off if stuck.
key_takeaways: Deadlock: two or more threads block each other permanently, each waiting for a lock the other holds
Four Coffman conditions must hold for deadlock: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
Prevention: always acquire multiple locks in the same consistent order
Use ReentrantLock's tryLock(timeout) to allow threads to back off and retry
Avoid acquiring nested locks wherever possible — redesign to use a single lock
Use thread dump (jstack) or tools like VisualVM to detect deadlocks in production
Higher-level concurrent utilities (Semaphore, BlockingQueue) reduce the risk of deadlocks

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Stream.map() vs Stream.flatMap()
questions.question: What is the difference between map() and flatMap() in Java Streams?
questions.answer: Both map() and flatMap() are intermediate Stream operations that transform elements, but they differ in how they handle the result: map() applies a function to each element and wraps the result in a one-to-one mapping — each input element produces exactly one output element. If the function returns a collection or stream, you get a Stream of collections. flatMap() applies a function that returns a Stream (or collection) for each element and then flattens all those streams into a single stream — effectively a one-to-many mapping followed by merging. The key rule: use map() when each element transforms to one value. Use flatMap() when each element transforms to multiple values (e.g., splitting a sentence into words, or unpacking a list of lists).
questions.explanation: Imagine you have a box of envelopes. map() lets you stamp each envelope — one envelope in, one stamped envelope out. flatMap() lets you open each envelope, take out all the letters inside, and put all the letters together in one pile — many letters come out per envelope, and they're all in one flat pile at the end.
key_takeaways: map(): one-to-one — each element maps to exactly one result
flatMap(): one-to-many — each element maps to a Stream/collection, then all are flattened into one stream
Use map() for simple transformations (uppercase, parse, multiply)
Use flatMap() to flatten nested structures (list of lists, splitting strings into characters)
flatMap() is essentially map() + flatten in one operation
Both are intermediate operations — no processing happens until a terminal operation is called
flatMap() requires the mapping function to return a Stream — use Arrays.stream() or Collection.stream()

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Collectors in Java Streams
questions.question: What are Collectors in Java Streams? Explain the most commonly used Collectors with examples.
questions.answer: Collectors is a utility class in java.util.stream that provides implementations of the Collector interface — used with the terminal operation collect() to accumulate stream elements into a result container. The most commonly used Collectors are: (1) toList() — collects into a List. (2) toSet() — collects into a Set (removes duplicates). (3) toMap(keyMapper, valueMapper) — collects into a Map. (4) joining() — concatenates strings. (5) groupingBy(classifier) — groups elements into a Map<K, List<V>>. (6) counting() — counts elements. (7) partitioningBy(predicate) — splits into a Map<Boolean, List<V>>. (8) summarizingInt() / averagingInt() / summingInt() — statistical operations. (9) toUnmodifiableList() / toUnmodifiableSet() — immutable collections (Java 10+). groupingBy is especially powerful for SQL-like GROUP BY operations on collections.
questions.explanation: Collectors are like different types of containers at the end of a factory conveyor belt. toList() is a regular box, toSet() is a box that rejects duplicates, toMap() is a filing cabinet organized by label, groupingBy() is a sorting machine that puts items in labeled bins, and joining() is a stapler that combines everything into one document.
key_takeaways: Collectors is used with collect() — the terminal operation that ends a stream and accumulates results
toList(), toSet(), toMap() are the most basic Collectors
joining() concatenates String elements — supports delimiter, prefix, suffix
groupingBy() groups elements by a classifier into Map<K, List<V>> — like SQL GROUP BY
partitioningBy() splits into exactly two groups (true/false) based on a predicate
Downstream collectors (counting, averaging, summarizing) can be nested inside groupingBy
Java 10+ adds toUnmodifiableList() and toUnmodifiableSet() for immutable results

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Encapsulation and its Benefits
questions.question: What is encapsulation in Java and why is it important?
questions.answer: Encapsulation is one of the four fundamental OOP principles. It refers to bundling data (fields) and the methods that operate on that data together in a class, while restricting direct access to the internal state from outside using access modifiers. In Java, encapsulation is implemented by: (1) Making fields private so they cannot be accessed directly from outside the class. (2) Providing public getter and setter methods to read and modify the fields in a controlled way. (3) Adding validation logic inside setters to protect data integrity. Benefits of encapsulation: Data hiding — internal implementation details are hidden; Controlled access — setters can validate data before assigning; Flexibility — internal implementation can change without affecting external code; Maintainability — each class manages its own state; Testability — well-encapsulated classes are easier to unit test.
questions.explanation: Encapsulation is like the dashboard of a car. You (the user) interact with the steering wheel, pedals, and buttons (public methods), but the engine, fuel system, and wiring (private fields) are hidden behind the dashboard. You don't need to know how the engine works to drive — and the manufacturer can upgrade the engine without changing your interface.
key_takeaways: Encapsulation: bundle data and methods together; restrict access using private fields + public getters/setters
Private fields cannot be accessed or modified directly from outside the class
Getters provide read access; setters provide write access with optional validation
Encapsulation protects data integrity — invalid states can be rejected in setters
It supports loose coupling — internal changes don't break external code
Not all fields need both getter and setter — expose only what's necessary
Java Records (Java 16+) provide a concise way to model immutable data with built-in accessors

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Polymorphism — Compile-time vs Runtime
questions.question: Explain polymorphism in Java. What is the difference between compile-time and runtime polymorphism?
questions.answer: Polymorphism means 'many forms' — it allows the same interface or method name to behave differently based on the context. Java supports two types: (1) Compile-time Polymorphism (Static Binding): Achieved through method overloading. The compiler determines which method to call at compile time based on the method signature (name + parameter types). (2) Runtime Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding): Achieved through method overriding and inheritance. When a parent class reference holds a child class object, the JVM determines the actual method to call at runtime based on the actual object type, not the reference type. This is the core of Java's dynamic dispatch. The instanceof operator and casting are also related concepts. Runtime polymorphism enables the Open/Closed Principle — code is open for extension but closed for modification.
questions.explanation: Polymorphism is like a remote control labeled 'PLAY'. On a DVD player, PLAY shows a movie. On a music system, PLAY plays a song. On a game console, PLAY starts a game. The button is the same, but the behavior differs based on which device you're using (which object type). That's runtime polymorphism in action.
key_takeaways: Polymorphism = same interface, different behavior depending on the object
Compile-time polymorphism: method overloading — resolved at compile time by method signature
Runtime polymorphism: method overriding — resolved at runtime based on actual object type
A parent reference can hold a child object — method calls are dispatched to the child's version
Runtime polymorphism enables flexible, extensible code (Open/Closed Principle)
Static methods are NOT polymorphic — they use static binding (compile-time)
Casting is required to access child-specific methods via a parent reference

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Functional Interfaces and Lambda Expressions
questions.question: What are functional interfaces in Java and how do lambda expressions relate to them?
questions.answer: A functional interface is an interface that has exactly one abstract method (SAM — Single Abstract Method). It can have any number of default or static methods. The @FunctionalInterface annotation enforces this constraint at compile time. Lambda expressions provide a concise way to implement functional interfaces — instead of creating an anonymous class, you write a short expression that represents the method body. Java 8 introduced the java.util.function package with built-in functional interfaces: Runnable (no args, no return), Supplier<T> (no args, returns T), Consumer<T> (takes T, no return), Function<T,R> (takes T, returns R), Predicate<T> (takes T, returns boolean), BiFunction<T,U,R> (takes T and U, returns R). Lambdas, combined with Streams and method references, form the backbone of modern functional-style Java programming.
questions.explanation: A functional interface is like a job posting with exactly one responsibility. A lambda expression is you saying 'I'll do that job' in shorthand — instead of writing a full job application (anonymous class), you just describe what you'll do (the method body). Java's built-in functional interfaces cover the most common 'job types': supply something, consume something, transform something, or test something.
key_takeaways: Functional interface: exactly one abstract method (SAM); use @FunctionalInterface to enforce
Lambda expression: concise syntax to implement a functional interface inline
java.util.function provides built-in functional interfaces: Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier, BiFunction
Method references (ClassName::method) are a shorthand for certain lambdas
Lambdas can capture effectively final local variables from the enclosing scope
Functional interfaces enable higher-order functions — passing behavior as arguments
Lambdas reduce boilerplate compared to anonymous classes

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Optional in Java
questions.question: What is Optional in Java and why was it introduced? How do you use it correctly?
questions.answer: Optional<T> is a container class introduced in Java 8 that may or may not contain a non-null value. It was introduced to address the common problem of NullPointerException — by making the absence of a value explicit in the type system rather than represented by null. Key methods: Optional.of(value) — creates an Optional with a non-null value (throws NPE if null); Optional.ofNullable(value) — creates an Optional that may contain null; Optional.empty() — creates an empty Optional; isPresent() / isEmpty() — checks if value exists; get() — retrieves the value (throws NoSuchElementException if empty — avoid using alone); orElse(default) — returns value or a default; orElseGet(Supplier) — returns value or a lazily computed default; orElseThrow() — throws an exception if empty; ifPresent(Consumer) — runs code if present; map() / filter() / flatMap() — transform the value inside Optional. Best practices: use Optional as a return type, never as a method parameter or field. Do not call get() without checking isPresent().
questions.explanation: Optional is like a gift box that might or might not contain something. Instead of handing someone a box and hoping they check before opening (risking NullPointerException), Optional forces you to ask: 'Is there something in here?' before taking it out — or provide a fallback in case it's empty.
key_takeaways: Optional<T> is a container that explicitly represents a potentially absent value
Introduced in Java 8 to reduce NullPointerExceptions and make absence explicit
Use Optional.ofNullable() when the value might be null; Optional.of() when it's guaranteed non-null
Prefer orElse(), orElseGet(), orElseThrow() over calling get() directly
Use Optional as a return type — not as a method parameter or class field
orElse() always evaluates the default; orElseGet() is lazy (only evaluates if empty) — prefer orElseGet for expensive defaults
Chain map(), filter(), flatMap() for clean transformation pipelines on Optional values

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Java Memory Model and Happens-Before
questions.question: What is the Java Memory Model (JMM) and what does the happens-before relationship guarantee?
questions.answer: The Java Memory Model (JMM), defined in the Java Language Specification, specifies how threads interact through memory and what behaviors are allowed in concurrent programs. It defines when writes made by one thread become visible to another. Without the JMM, the JVM and hardware can reorder instructions for performance (compiler reordering, CPU out-of-order execution, cache effects). The happens-before (HB) relationship is the core concept of the JMM. If action A happens-before action B, then the effects of A are guaranteed to be visible to B. Key happens-before guarantees: (1) Program order rule — each action in a thread happens-before every subsequent action in that thread. (2) Monitor lock rule — unlocking a monitor happens-before any subsequent lock of the same monitor. (3) Volatile variable rule — a write to a volatile field happens-before every subsequent read of that field. (4) Thread start rule — Thread.start() happens-before any action in the started thread. (5) Thread join rule — all actions in a thread happen-before Thread.join() returns. (6) Transitivity — if A HB B and B HB C, then A HB C.
questions.explanation: The JMM is a contract between the programmer and the JVM. Without it, the JVM is free to reorder, cache, or optimize operations in ways that break multithreaded programs. Happens-before is the guarantee that certain memory writes will be visible across threads. Think of it as a timestamp system: if A is stamped before B, everything A wrote is readable when B runs.
key_takeaways: JMM defines rules for how thread memory interactions behave — without it, reads can see stale values
Happens-before (HB): if A HB B, then all writes by A are visible to B
Program order, monitor unlock, volatile write, thread.start(), thread.join() all create HB edges
Volatile variable rule: a volatile write HB any subsequent volatile read of the same variable
HB is transitive: A HB B and B HB C implies A HB C
Data races occur when two threads access the same variable without a HB relationship and at least one is a write
synchronized, volatile, and java.util.concurrent classes all establish happens-before guarantees

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: try-with-resources
questions.question: What is try-with-resources in Java and why is it preferred over traditional try-finally?
questions.answer: try-with-resources (introduced in Java 7) is a special form of the try statement that automatically closes resources (like files, streams, connections) when the try block exits — whether normally or due to an exception. It requires the resource to implement the AutoCloseable (or Closeable) interface, which defines the close() method. In traditional try-finally, you must manually call close() in the finally block, which is verbose and error-prone — especially because an exception thrown in finally can suppress the original exception. With try-with-resources: resources are declared in the try() header, are closed automatically in reverse order of declaration, and if both the try block and close() throw exceptions, the close() exception is suppressed (accessible via Throwable.getSuppressed()) and the original exception propagates. Multiple resources can be declared separated by semicolons.
questions.explanation: Traditional try-finally is like opening a book, reading it, and having to remember to close it yourself — and if you forget, or if closing it causes a problem, your original work could be lost. try-with-resources is like a library book with a self-closing cover — it closes automatically when you're done, without you having to remember.
key_takeaways: try-with-resources automatically closes AutoCloseable resources when the block exits
Resources declared in try() are closed in reverse order of declaration
Eliminates the need for verbose and error-prone try-finally cleanup code
If both try block and close() throw, the close() exception is suppressed — original propagates
Suppressed exceptions are accessible via Throwable.getSuppressed()
Resource must implement AutoCloseable or Closeable
Introduced in Java 7; multiple resources supported; Java 9 allows effectively final variables in try()

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Design Patterns — Singleton
questions.question: What is the Singleton design pattern in Java? Explain thread-safe implementations.
questions.answer: The Singleton pattern ensures that a class has only one instance throughout the application lifecycle and provides a global access point to it. Common use cases: logging, configuration managers, connection pools, caches. Naive implementations using a simple if-null check are not thread-safe — two threads can simultaneously check null and both create instances. Thread-safe implementations: (1) Synchronized method — simplest, but slow due to locking on every call. (2) Double-Checked Locking (DCL) — checks the instance twice with a synchronized block only on first creation. The instance field must be volatile to prevent partial initialization visibility issues. (3) Static Inner Class (Bill Pugh Singleton) — the most recommended approach. Uses class loading guarantee: the inner class is loaded only when getInstance() is called, and class loading is thread-safe by the JVM. (4) Enum Singleton — the simplest and safest — handles serialization and reflection attacks automatically.
questions.explanation: Singleton is like a country having exactly one president — no matter who asks, it's always the same person. The tricky part in a multithreaded country is making sure two departments don't accidentally elect two presidents at the same time. The Bill Pugh inner class approach is like a voting system that only activates when needed and is guaranteed to elect exactly one president by design.
key_takeaways: Singleton: only one instance per JVM, globally accessible via getInstance()
Naive lazy initialization is not thread-safe — two threads can create two instances
Synchronized method: thread-safe but slow — locks on every call
Double-Checked Locking: fast; volatile is required to prevent partial initialization
Bill Pugh (static inner class): recommended — leverages JVM class-loading thread safety
Enum Singleton: simplest and safest — immune to reflection and serialization attacks
Private constructor prevents external instantiation

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: ConcurrentHashMap vs Collections.synchronizedMap
questions.question: What is the difference between ConcurrentHashMap and Collections.synchronizedMap in Java?
questions.answer: Both provide thread-safe Map implementations, but they differ significantly in implementation and performance: Collections.synchronizedMap(map) wraps any map and synchronizes every method by locking on the entire map object. Only one thread can access the map at a time — even for reads. This is a coarse-grained lock. ConcurrentHashMap (Java 5+) uses a much more sophisticated mechanism. In Java 7 it used Segment-level locking (16 segments by default). In Java 8+ it uses bucket-level CAS (Compare-And-Swap) operations and synchronized only on individual bucket heads — allowing multiple threads to read and write concurrently to different buckets. Key differences: (1) Locking granularity — synchronizedMap: entire map; ConcurrentHashMap: bucket-level. (2) Read operations — synchronizedMap locks for reads; ConcurrentHashMap reads are mostly lock-free. (3) Null support — ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null keys or values; synchronizedMap depends on the backing map. (4) Iteration — iterating synchronizedMap requires external synchronization; ConcurrentHashMap's iterator is weakly consistent (doesn't throw ConcurrentModificationException). (5) Atomic operations — ConcurrentHashMap supports atomic operations like putIfAbsent(), computeIfAbsent().
questions.explanation: synchronizedMap is like a library where only one person can enter at a time — even just to look at titles. ConcurrentHashMap is like a library with many aisles (buckets) where multiple people can browse different sections simultaneously, and only the person changing a specific shelf needs exclusive access to that shelf.
key_takeaways: synchronizedMap: coarse-grained lock — entire map locked per operation, even reads
ConcurrentHashMap: fine-grained — bucket-level locking in Java 7, CAS in Java 8+
ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and parallel writes to different buckets
ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null keys or values; synchronizedMap depends on backing map
ConcurrentHashMap iteration is weakly consistent — no ConcurrentModificationException
Prefer ConcurrentHashMap over synchronizedMap or Hashtable in multithreaded code
ConcurrentHashMap provides atomic compound operations: putIfAbsent, computeIfAbsent, merge

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Parallel Streams
questions.question: What are parallel streams in Java? When should you use them and what are their pitfalls?
questions.answer: Parallel streams allow stream operations to be executed concurrently using multiple CPU cores. They split the data source into chunks using the ForkJoinPool (specifically the common ForkJoinPool), process each chunk in parallel, and then combine the results. You can create a parallel stream with collection.parallelStream() or stream.parallel(). When to use parallel streams: computationally intensive operations (CPU-bound work), large datasets where the overhead of parallelism is justified, stateless operations (no shared mutable state), operations that can be safely decomposed and merged. Pitfalls and when NOT to use parallel streams: (1) Small datasets — the overhead of thread management exceeds the benefit. (2) Ordered operations — maintaining order is expensive in parallel (use unordered() for speedup if order doesn't matter). (3) Shared mutable state — parallel streams and side effects cause race conditions. (4) I/O-bound work — threads block on I/O, no benefit. (5) Non-associative reduction operations may produce incorrect results. (6) All parallel streams share the common ForkJoinPool — one heavy task can starve others.
questions.explanation: Parallel streams are like splitting a big document to proofread among a team. If the document is large enough, splitting saves time. But if it's just one page, gathering everyone together and dividing it actually takes longer than one person doing it. Also, if team members share a single pen (shared mutable state), they'll get in each other's way and cause errors.
key_takeaways: Parallel streams split work across multiple cores using the common ForkJoinPool
Use parallelStream() or stream().parallel() to enable parallelism
Best for large, CPU-bound, stateless operations that can be safely split and merged
Avoid parallel streams for small datasets, I/O-bound work, or operations with shared mutable state
Side effects in parallel streams cause race conditions — never modify a shared list directly
Use collect() with Collectors for thread-safe result accumulation in parallel streams
All parallel streams share the common ForkJoinPool — avoid blocking tasks that could starve other streams

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Abstract Class vs Interface
questions.question: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface in Java?
questions.answer: An abstract class is a class that cannot be instantiated and may contain a mix of abstract methods (no body) and concrete methods (with body). It can have constructors, instance variables, and any access modifier on its members. A class can extend only one abstract class (single inheritance). An interface is a contract that defines a set of abstract methods that implementing classes must provide. Since Java 8, interfaces can also have default methods (with body) and static methods. Since Java 9, interfaces can have private methods. Interfaces cannot have instance variables — only public static final constants. A class can implement multiple interfaces (multiple inheritance of type). Key rule: use an abstract class when you want to share code among closely related classes; use an interface when you want to define a contract that unrelated classes can implement.
questions.explanation: Think of an abstract class as a partially built house — it has a foundation, some walls, and a skeleton (concrete methods), but some rooms are left empty for you to finish (abstract methods). An interface is like a blueprint contract — it just lists requirements ('must have 3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms') without providing any structure. A class is the builder who must follow the interface contract or extend the partially built house.
key_takeaways: Abstract class: can have fields, constructors, concrete + abstract methods; single inheritance only
Interface: only constants and method signatures (+ default/static since Java 8); supports multiple implementation
A class can extend only ONE abstract class but implement MANY interfaces
Abstract class is for 'is-a' relationship with shared code; interface is for 'can-do' contract
Since Java 8, interfaces can have default and static methods — reducing the gap with abstract classes
Abstract class constructors run during subclass object creation even though abstract class can't be instantiated directly
Choose interface when designing API contracts; choose abstract class when sharing common implementation

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: static Keyword in Java
questions.question: What does the static keyword mean in Java? Explain its use with variables, methods, blocks, and nested classes.
questions.answer: The static keyword in Java means that a member belongs to the class itself rather than to any specific instance (object) of the class. Static members are shared across all instances. (1) Static variables (class variables): There is only one copy shared by all objects. Changing it via one object affects all others. Stored in the Method Area of the JVM. (2) Static methods: Can be called on the class directly without creating an object (ClassName.method()). Cannot use 'this' or access instance (non-static) members directly. (3) Static blocks: Run once when the class is first loaded into the JVM, before any constructor. Used for static initialization (e.g., loading configuration). (4) Static nested classes: A nested class declared static. Does not need an instance of the outer class to be instantiated. Cannot access non-static members of the outer class directly.
questions.explanation: Imagine a company with 500 employees. Each employee has their own name and salary (instance variables). But the company name is one thing shared by all — that's a static variable. A static method is like an HR announcement board — you don't need to talk to a specific employee to read it. A static block is the company's one-time orientation session — runs once when the company opens.
key_takeaways: static members belong to the class, not to any instance — shared by all objects
Static variables have one copy for the entire class; instance variables are per-object
Static methods cannot access 'this', instance variables, or instance methods directly
Static blocks run once when the class is first loaded — before any constructor
Static nested classes do not require an outer class instance to be created
Static methods are resolved at compile time (static binding) — they cannot be overridden (only hidden)
Common static uses: utility/helper classes (Math, Arrays), constants (static final), factory methods

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Inheritance in Java
questions.question: What is inheritance in Java? How does it work and what are its types and limitations?
questions.answer: Inheritance is an OOP mechanism where one class (subclass/child) acquires the properties and behaviors (fields and methods) of another class (superclass/parent). In Java, inheritance is implemented using the extends keyword. The child class inherits all non-private members of the parent class and can: override parent methods to provide its own implementation, add new fields and methods, and call parent methods using super. Java supports: (1) Single Inheritance — one class inherits from one parent. (2) Multilevel Inheritance — A extends B, B extends C. (3) Hierarchical Inheritance — multiple classes extend the same parent. Java does NOT support multiple class inheritance (a class cannot extend two classes) to avoid the Diamond Problem. However, multiple inheritance is supported via interfaces. The Object class is the root of the entire Java class hierarchy — every class implicitly extends Object.
questions.explanation: Inheritance is like a family tree. A child inherits the family surname, traits, and some belongings (fields and methods) from their parents. They can use those traits as-is, modify some (override), and add their own unique traits. Just as you can have one biological family (single inheritance) but be a member of multiple clubs (interfaces), Java classes extend one parent but implement many interfaces.
key_takeaways: Inheritance: child class (extends) acquires fields and methods from parent class
Java supports single, multilevel, and hierarchical inheritance — NOT multiple class inheritance
Use super() to call parent constructor; super.method() to call parent's version of overridden method
Every Java class implicitly extends Object — the root of the class hierarchy
Private members of parent are inherited but not directly accessible in child
Multiple inheritance is achieved through interfaces (implements), not extends
Inheritance promotes code reuse but should follow the 'is-a' relationship (Dog IS-A Animal)

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Autoboxing and Unboxing
questions.question: What are autoboxing and unboxing in Java? What are their benefits and potential pitfalls?
questions.answer: Autoboxing is the automatic conversion that Java performs from a primitive type to its corresponding wrapper class object. Unboxing is the reverse — automatic conversion from a wrapper object back to a primitive. Java introduced this in Java 5 to allow primitives to be used where objects are required (e.g., in Collections). The wrapper classes are: int → Integer, double → Double, boolean → Boolean, char → Character, long → Long, byte → Byte, short → Short, float → Float. Autoboxing/unboxing happens automatically in assignments, method calls, and expressions. Pitfalls: (1) Performance — autoboxing creates new objects; heavy use in loops can be expensive. (2) NullPointerException — unboxing a null wrapper throws NPE. (3) Integer cache — Integer values from -128 to 127 are cached; == on cached Integers works, but outside that range, == compares references (not values). Always use .equals() for Integer comparison.
questions.explanation: Autoboxing is like an automatic gift-wrapping service. When you need to put a number (primitive) into a collection box (Collection), Java automatically wraps it in a box (wrapper object). When you take it out and use it in math, Java automatically unwraps it. The danger is picking up an empty box (null) and trying to unwrap it — that causes a NullPointerException.
key_takeaways: Autoboxing: automatic conversion from primitive (int) to wrapper (Integer)
Unboxing: automatic conversion from wrapper (Integer) to primitive (int)
Allows primitives to be used in Collections, generics, and anywhere objects are required
Unboxing a null wrapper object causes NullPointerException
Integer cache: values -128 to 127 are cached — == works accidentally; outside range, == compares references
Always use .equals() to compare Integer (and other wrapper) objects
Autoboxing in tight loops creates many objects — use primitives or primitive arrays for performance-critical code

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Stack vs Queue
questions.question: What are Stack and Queue in Java? When would you use each and what implementations are available?
questions.answer: Stack and Queue are two fundamental data structures with opposite ordering behaviors. Stack follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) — the last element added is the first one removed. Like a stack of plates: you always take from the top. Queue follows FIFO (First In, First Out) — the first element added is the first one removed. Like a line of people: the first person to arrive is served first. In Java: Stack class (legacy, extends Vector) is available but not recommended. The preferred approach for stack behavior is to use Deque implementations: ArrayDeque (faster, not thread-safe) or LinkedList. For Queue: the Queue interface is implemented by LinkedList, ArrayDeque, and PriorityQueue. PriorityQueue is special — elements are dequeued in natural order (or custom Comparator), not insertion order. For thread-safe queues: ArrayBlockingQueue, LinkedBlockingQueue from java.util.concurrent.
questions.explanation: Stack is like a pile of books — you put a book on top (push) and remove from the top (pop). Queue is like a checkout line at a supermarket — you join at the back (enqueue) and are served from the front (dequeue). Knowing which one to use depends on the order you need: most recent first (Stack) vs. first come, first served (Queue).
key_takeaways: Stack: LIFO (Last In First Out) — push/pop from the top
Queue: FIFO (First In First Out) — enqueue at back, dequeue from front
Use ArrayDeque for both stack and queue behavior — it's faster than LinkedList
Avoid the legacy Stack class (extends Vector, synchronized overhead)
PriorityQueue dequeues in natural sorted order (min-heap by default), not insertion order
Queue methods: offer() (add), poll() (remove from front), peek() (look without removing)
For thread-safe queues, use BlockingQueue implementations from java.util.concurrent

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: throw vs throws in Java
questions.question: What is the difference between throw and throws in Java?
questions.answer: throw and throws are both related to exception handling but serve very different purposes. throw is a statement used inside a method body to explicitly raise (throw) an exception. You throw a single specific exception instance: throw new IllegalArgumentException("message"). Execution of the current method stops at the throw statement and the exception propagates up the call stack. throws is a keyword used in a method signature to declare that the method might throw certain checked exceptions. It is a warning to the caller: 'this method may throw these exceptions — you must handle or re-declare them'. It does not throw anything by itself — it's just a declaration. Key differences: throw actually throws the exception; throws declares possible exceptions. throw appears in the method body; throws appears in the method signature. throw takes one exception instance; throws can list multiple exception types separated by commas.
questions.explanation: throw is the action of actually throwing a ball (the exception). throws is a warning label on a machine that says 'this machine may throw balls — wear protective gear!' You act (throw) with throw; you warn (declare) with throws. The caller sees throws and must decide whether to catch the ball or let it pass through to their own caller.
key_takeaways: throw: a statement inside a method body that actually raises an exception instance
throws: a keyword in the method signature that declares possible checked exceptions
throw takes exactly one exception object; throws lists one or more exception types
throw stops execution and propagates the exception up the call stack
throws tells the caller: 'you must handle or re-declare these exceptions'
Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException) can be thrown without declaring them in throws
You can use throw with checked or unchecked exceptions; throws is only relevant for checked exceptions

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Generics in Java
questions.question: What are generics in Java? Explain type parameters, bounded types, and wildcards.
questions.answer: Generics allow you to write classes, interfaces, and methods that work with any data type while providing compile-time type safety. They eliminate the need for explicit casting and catch type mismatches at compile time rather than runtime. Syntax: Box<T> defines a generic class where T is a type parameter. At usage time, Box<String> specifies the concrete type. Bounded type parameters restrict the allowed types: T extends Number means T must be Number or a subtype. Wildcards (?) represent an unknown type: (1) Unbounded wildcard: List<?> — can be any type; useful when type doesn't matter. (2) Upper bounded wildcard: List<? extends Number> — accepts Number or any subtype (read-only). (3) Lower bounded wildcard: List<? super Integer> — accepts Integer or any supertype (write-friendly). Type erasure: Generic type information is erased at runtime — the JVM only sees raw types. This means you cannot create generic arrays or use instanceof with generic types.
questions.explanation: Generics are like a labeled container. Instead of a generic box that holds 'anything' and you have to guess what's inside (Object with casting), a Box<Apple> tells you upfront: 'only Apples here.' Wildcards are like saying 'a box of some fruit' — you know it's fruit but not which exact kind. Upper bounded ('? extends Fruit') lets you read fruit from the box; lower bounded ('? super Apple') lets you put Apples into a box of something.
key_takeaways: Generics provide compile-time type safety and eliminate the need for explicit casting
Type parameter T is a placeholder replaced with a concrete type at usage (Box<String>)
Bounded types: <T extends Number> restricts T to Number and its subtypes
Upper bounded wildcard (? extends T): used when reading — can read T, cannot add elements
Lower bounded wildcard (? super T): used when writing — can add T, reading returns Object
Type erasure: generic type info is erased at runtime — JVM sees raw types (List, not List<String>)
Cannot create generic arrays (new T[]) or use instanceof with generic types due to type erasure

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: synchronized Keyword in Java
questions.question: What does the synchronized keyword do in Java? Explain synchronized methods vs synchronized blocks.
questions.answer: The synchronized keyword is Java's built-in mechanism for mutual exclusion — ensuring that only one thread at a time executes a critical section of code. Every Java object has an intrinsic lock (monitor). When a thread enters a synchronized method or block, it acquires the object's lock. Other threads trying to enter any synchronized section on the same object are blocked until the lock is released. Synchronized method: entire method body is synchronized; locks on 'this' for instance methods, or on the class object (ClassName.class) for static methods. Synchronized block: locks only the specified section of code; more granular control. You specify exactly which object to lock on. Advantages of synchronized blocks over synchronized methods: (1) Less blocking — other non-critical code in the method can run without waiting for the lock. (2) Flexibility — can lock on different objects for independent data. (3) Better performance — shorter critical sections mean less contention.
questions.explanation: synchronized is like a single bathroom in a shared office — only one person can be inside at a time. A synchronized method is like locking the entire floor when you use the bathroom. A synchronized block is like locking only the bathroom door — other floors (other code) remain accessible. The key is: lock only what you must protect, for as short a time as possible.
key_takeaways: synchronized ensures only one thread executes a critical section at a time — mutual exclusion
Every object has an intrinsic lock (monitor); synchronized acquires and releases this lock
Synchronized instance method locks on 'this'; synchronized static method locks on Class object
Synchronized block allows finer control — specify exactly which object to lock on
Prefer synchronized blocks over entire synchronized methods for better performance
Lock on the smallest scope necessary — long-held locks cause thread contention and reduce throughput
synchronized provides mutual exclusion AND memory visibility (happens-before guarantee)

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Lazy Evaluation in Java Streams
questions.question: What is lazy evaluation in Java Streams? How do intermediate and terminal operations differ?
questions.answer: Java Streams use lazy evaluation — intermediate operations are not executed immediately when called. Instead, they build a pipeline of transformations. Execution only begins when a terminal operation is invoked. This is a key performance feature. Intermediate operations (lazy): return a new Stream and can be chained. Examples: filter(), map(), sorted(), distinct(), limit(), skip(), peek(), flatMap(). They are not executed until a terminal operation is called. Terminal operations (eager): trigger the pipeline to actually execute and produce a result or side effect. Examples: collect(), forEach(), count(), reduce(), findFirst(), anyMatch(), allMatch(), min(), max(), toList(). Short-circuit operations: Some terminal operations (findFirst(), anyMatch(), limit()) can stop processing early — they don't need to process every element. Combined with laziness, filter()+findFirst() on a large list only processes elements until a match is found, not the entire list.
questions.explanation: Lazy evaluation is like a food delivery app. When you browse items (intermediate operations), nothing is actually cooked yet — you're just building your order. Only when you click 'Place Order' (terminal operation) does the kitchen start cooking. Short-circuit is like saying 'I just need the first available pizza' — the kitchen stops as soon as one pizza is ready, rather than cooking them all.
key_takeaways: Intermediate operations (filter, map, sorted) are lazy — they don't execute until a terminal operation is called
Terminal operations (collect, forEach, count, reduce) trigger actual pipeline execution
Lazy evaluation avoids unnecessary processing — only elements needed for the result are processed
Short-circuit operations (findFirst, anyMatch, limit) can stop the pipeline early
filter() + findFirst() on a large list processes only until a match is found — very efficient
A stream pipeline can only be consumed once — calling a terminal operation a second time throws IllegalStateException
peek() is an intermediate operation useful for debugging without affecting pipeline output

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Class Loading in Java
questions.question: How does class loading work in Java? Explain the ClassLoader hierarchy and the delegation model.
questions.answer: Class loading is the process by which the JVM reads a .class file and creates a Class object in memory. The Java ClassLoader system uses a hierarchy of three built-in class loaders: (1) Bootstrap ClassLoader: The root loader, implemented in native code (C/C++). Loads core Java classes from the JRE/lib directory (java.lang, java.util, etc.). Has no parent — it IS the root. (2) Extension ClassLoader (Platform ClassLoader in Java 9+): Loads classes from the JRE/lib/ext directory and javax packages. (3) Application ClassLoader (System ClassLoader): Loads classes from the application's classpath (your .class files and JARs). Parent delegation model: When a class loader is asked to load a class, it first delegates to its parent. Only if the parent cannot find the class does the child attempt to load it. This ensures that core Java classes cannot be overridden by user classes with the same name. Class loading phases: Loading (read bytecode), Linking (Verify → Prepare → Resolve), Initialization (run static initializers).
questions.explanation: The ClassLoader hierarchy is like a chain of command in an organization. When a task comes in (load a class), it goes to the top manager first (Bootstrap). If the top manager can handle it (core Java class), done. If not, it passes down to middle management (Extension), then to the employee (Application ClassLoader). This ensures core responsibilities (java.lang.String) are always handled by the most trusted authority, not by anyone who claims to handle it.
key_takeaways: ClassLoaders load .class files into JVM memory as Class objects
Three built-in loaders: Bootstrap (core Java) → Extension/Platform (javax) → Application (your code)
Parent delegation model: always ask parent first before attempting to load — prevents core class hijacking
Bootstrap ClassLoader returns null in Java since it has no Java representation (it's native code)
Class loading phases: Loading → Linking (Verify, Prepare, Resolve) → Initialization
Static initializers run during the Initialization phase — once per class, per ClassLoader
Custom ClassLoaders enable plugin architectures, hot code reloading, and sandboxing

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Comparable vs Comparator
questions.question: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator in Java? When do you use each?
questions.answer: Both Comparable and Comparator are used to define ordering for objects, but they serve different purposes. Comparable (java.lang.Comparable<T>): Defines the natural (default) ordering of a class. Implemented by the class itself via the compareTo(T other) method. A class can have only one natural ordering. Used automatically by Collections.sort(), Arrays.sort(), TreeSet, TreeMap, etc. when no Comparator is provided. Comparator (java.util.Comparator<T>): Defines a custom, external ordering. Implemented separately from the class (can be a lambda). A class can have multiple Comparators for different orderings (by name, by age, by salary). Passed explicitly to sort methods and sorted collections. Key rule: use Comparable when there is one obvious natural ordering (e.g., numbers, dates, alphabetical). Use Comparator when you need multiple orderings, you can't modify the class, or you need a different order than the natural one.
questions.explanation: Comparable is like giving everyone a default rank (e.g., by employee ID). Every employee knows their rank relative to others by default. Comparator is like a flexible ranking system — HR can sort employees by salary today, by join date tomorrow, by department next week. The rules change without changing the employee records themselves.
key_takeaways: Comparable: implemented BY the class; defines one natural ordering via compareTo()
Comparator: implemented SEPARATELY; defines custom/alternative orderings via compare()
A class can have only one Comparable ordering; multiple Comparators can be created for the same class
Collections.sort() and Arrays.sort() use Comparable by default; pass a Comparator for custom order
Comparator.comparing(), comparingInt(), thenComparing() allow readable method-chaining
Use Comparable when there's an obvious single natural order; Comparator for flexibility
compareTo() / compare() convention: negative if a < b, zero if a == b, positive if a > b

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Java Reflection API
questions.question: What is the Reflection API in Java? What can you do with it and what are its drawbacks?
questions.answer: The Java Reflection API (java.lang.reflect) allows a program to inspect, analyze, and modify its own structure and behavior at runtime — even accessing private fields and methods. With reflection, you can: (1) Inspect class metadata: class name, superclass, interfaces, fields, methods, constructors, annotations. (2) Create objects dynamically: Class.forName("ClassName").getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance(). (3) Invoke methods dynamically: method.invoke(object, args). (4) Access and modify fields: field.setAccessible(true); field.set(object, value) — even private fields. Use cases: frameworks (Spring, Hibernate, JUnit), serialization libraries, dependency injection containers, ORM tools, testing utilities. Drawbacks: (1) Performance overhead — reflection is significantly slower than direct code. (2) Security risk — bypasses access modifiers (private, final). (3) Breaks encapsulation — exposes internal implementation details. (4) Compile-time safety lost — errors appear at runtime instead of compile time. (5) Code is harder to read and maintain.
questions.explanation: Reflection is like having an X-ray machine for your code. At runtime, you can look inside any class and see its skeleton (fields, methods), or even change what's inside (modify private fields). Frameworks like Spring use this to wire your objects together without you writing explicit code. But just like X-rays, you should use it judiciously — exposing things that are meant to be private has consequences.
key_takeaways: Reflection allows runtime inspection and modification of classes, fields, methods, and constructors
setAccessible(true) bypasses private/protected access modifiers — use with extreme caution
Use cases: frameworks (Spring DI), ORM (Hibernate), testing (JUnit), serialization libraries
Reflection is significantly slower than direct code — avoid in performance-critical paths
Loses compile-time type safety — errors surface at runtime
Breaks encapsulation — private fields can be read and modified from outside the class
Java 9+ module system (JPMS) restricts illegal reflective access across module boundaries

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: ExecutorService and Thread Pools
questions.question: What is ExecutorService in Java? Why use thread pools instead of creating raw threads?
questions.answer: ExecutorService is a higher-level Java concurrency framework (java.util.concurrent) that manages a pool of threads for executing tasks asynchronously. Instead of manually creating and managing threads (new Thread()), you submit tasks (Runnable or Callable) to an ExecutorService and it handles scheduling, reuse, and lifecycle management. Why thread pools over raw threads: (1) Thread creation is expensive — threads require OS resources. Pools reuse existing threads. (2) Uncontrolled thread creation can exhaust system resources. Pools cap the number of concurrent threads. (3) ExecutorService provides better task management — submit, track, cancel, get results via Future. Common Executors factory methods: newFixedThreadPool(n) — fixed number of threads; newCachedThreadPool() — grows as needed, reuses idle threads; newSingleThreadExecutor() — one thread, tasks queued sequentially; newScheduledThreadPool(n) — schedule tasks with delay or periodic execution. Always shut down the ExecutorService (shutdown() + awaitTermination()) to release resources.
questions.explanation: Creating a raw thread for every task is like hiring a new employee for every single task, and firing them when done. It's wasteful and expensive. An ExecutorService with a thread pool is like having a team of workers (fixed pool) who pick up tasks from a queue, finish them, and immediately pick up the next one — efficient reuse without constant hiring and firing.
key_takeaways: ExecutorService manages a thread pool — submit tasks (Runnable/Callable) instead of creating raw threads
Thread pools reuse threads — avoids expensive thread creation/destruction overhead
newFixedThreadPool(n): fixed n threads; newCachedThreadPool(): grows/shrinks dynamically
submit() returns a Future — use future.get() to wait for and retrieve the result
invokeAll() submits a batch of Callables and returns all Futures when all complete
Always call shutdown() (and optionally awaitTermination()) to gracefully stop the pool
