Use ScheduledExecutorService for periodic or delayed task execution

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: reduce() in Java Streams
questions.question: What is the reduce() operation in Java Streams? How does it work and when should you use it?
questions.answer: reduce() is a terminal stream operation that combines all elements of a stream into a single result by repeatedly applying a binary operator. It is the foundation of many aggregate operations (sum, product, max, min, concatenation). There are three overloads: (1) reduce(identity, BinaryOperator) — takes an identity value (starting value / neutral element) and a combining function. Always returns T (never empty). (2) reduce(BinaryOperator) — no identity value. Returns Optional<T> because the stream might be empty. (3) reduce(identity, BiFunction, BinaryOperator) — for parallel streams where the accumulator and combiner can differ. The identity value must truly be a neutral element for the operation: 0 for addition, 1 for multiplication, empty string for concatenation, Integer.MIN_VALUE for max. Built-in aggregations like sum(), count(), min(), max() are more readable than reduce() for simple cases, but reduce() is flexible for custom operations.
questions.explanation: reduce() is like folding a list of numbers into one value. Start with an initial result (identity), and for each element, apply a rule: 'new result = combine(current result, element)'. For sum: start at 0, add each number: 0 + 1 = 1, 1 + 2 = 3, 3 + 3 = 6... Like folding a paper repeatedly — each fold combines the current state with the next piece.
key_takeaways: reduce() combines all stream elements into one result using a BinaryOperator
reduce(identity, op): always returns T; returns identity value if stream is empty
reduce(op): returns Optional<T>; empty Optional if stream is empty
Identity must be the neutral element for the operation (0 for sum, 1 for product, "" for concat)
Built-in methods (sum(), count(), min(), max()) are preferred over reduce() for simple aggregations
reduce() is the generalized form — use it for custom folding logic not covered by built-ins
For parallel streams, prefer the 3-arg reduce() with a separate combiner for correctness

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Design Patterns — Factory and Builder
questions.question: What are the Factory and Builder design patterns in Java? When and why do you use each?
questions.answer: Factory Pattern: A creational pattern that provides a method for creating objects without specifying the exact class to instantiate. Instead of calling new ConcreteClass(), the client calls a factory method that returns an object of the appropriate type based on parameters. Promotes loose coupling — the client depends on the interface/abstract type, not the concrete implementation. Variants: Simple Factory (static method), Factory Method (abstract method in subclass), Abstract Factory (families of related objects). Builder Pattern: A creational pattern used when constructing a complex object with many optional parameters. Instead of telescoping constructors (constructor with every combination of parameters), you use a Builder object that lets you set parameters step by step via a fluent API, then call build() to create the final object. Advantages: readable code, optional parameters, immutable objects. Used heavily in Java: StringBuilder, Stream.Builder, Lombok's @Builder, various framework configurations.
questions.explanation: Factory is like a vending machine — you choose what you want (press a button / pass a parameter) and the machine (factory) gives you the right product without you knowing how it was made. Builder is like customizing a burger — you tell the chef step by step: 'add cheese, no onions, extra sauce', and then the chef assembles and gives you the final burger when you say 'done'.
key_takeaways: Factory: centralizes object creation — client gets the right object without knowing the concrete class
Factory promotes loose coupling — client depends on interface, not implementation
Builder: constructs complex objects step-by-step via a fluent API, then calls build()
Builder solves the 'telescoping constructor' problem with many optional parameters
Builder enables immutable objects — all fields set before construction, no setters needed
Both are creational patterns from the Gang of Four (GoF) design patterns catalog
Real-world Java: StringBuilder, HttpClient.Builder, Stream.Builder, Lombok @Builder

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Iterator and Iterable
questions.question: What are the Iterator and Iterable interfaces in Java? How do they work with the for-each loop?
questions.answer: Iterable<T> is an interface with a single method: iterator(). Any class that implements Iterable can be used in a for-each (enhanced for) loop. Collections like ArrayList, LinkedList, HashSet, and even arrays all support for-each because they implement Iterable (or are arrays). Iterator<T> is an interface with three methods: hasNext() — returns true if more elements remain; next() — returns the next element; remove() — removes the last element returned by next() (optional operation). The for-each loop is syntactic sugar — the compiler translates it into an explicit Iterator loop. Key behavior: fail-fast iterators (ArrayList, HashMap) throw ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is structurally modified during iteration (except via Iterator.remove()). Java 8+ added forEach(Consumer) and removeIf(Predicate) as convenient alternatives to explicit iteration. ListIterator extends Iterator with bidirectional navigation (hasPrevious(), previous(), set(), add()).
questions.explanation: Iterable is like a library catalogue — it knows how to give you a reading guide. Iterator is the reading guide itself — it tracks your position and tells you 'is there a next book?' and 'give me the next book.' The for-each loop is like saying 'I'll read every book in this catalogue' — Java translates that into using the iterator behind the scenes.
key_takeaways: Iterable<T>: implement iterator() to make your class work with for-each loops
Iterator<T>: hasNext(), next(), remove() — tracks position during iteration
for-each loop is syntactic sugar for explicit Iterator usage — compiled into an iterator loop
Never structurally modify a collection during for-each — use Iterator.remove() instead
Modifying a fail-fast collection during iteration throws ConcurrentModificationException
Java 8+: removeIf(Predicate) and forEach(Consumer) offer cleaner alternatives to manual iteration
ListIterator extends Iterator with bidirectional traversal (previous(), set(), add())

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Java Records
questions.question: What are Records in Java? How do they differ from regular classes and when should you use them?
questions.answer: Records (introduced in Java 16, preview since Java 14) are a special kind of class designed to be transparent carriers of immutable data. They dramatically reduce boilerplate for data-holding classes. Declaring record Point(int x, int y) {} automatically generates: a canonical constructor with all fields as parameters, private final fields for each component, public accessor methods (x() and y(), not getX()), equals() and hashCode() based on all fields, toString() that includes all fields. Records are implicitly final (cannot be extended), cannot declare instance fields beyond components, and their components are implicitly final (immutable). Records CAN: implement interfaces, have static fields and methods, define additional constructors (must delegate to canonical), and override generated methods. Records CANNOT: extend other classes, be abstract, have non-final instance fields. Records are ideal for: DTOs (Data Transfer Objects), value objects, Tuple-like structures, return multiple values from a method.
questions.explanation: Before Records, creating a simple data class required: constructor, getters, equals(), hashCode(), and toString() — often 50+ lines for 2-3 fields. Records collapse all that into one line. They're like POJOs (Plain Old Java Objects) with batteries included — you declare the data, Java handles all the plumbing automatically.
key_takeaways: Records auto-generate: constructor, final fields, accessors (name(), not getName()), equals(), hashCode(), toString()
Records are implicitly final — cannot be extended or be abstract
Record components are immutable (private final) — designed for immutable data carriers
Compact constructor: validate/transform fields without full parameter list — components assigned automatically
Records can implement interfaces, have static members, and additional methods
Records cannot have non-record instance fields — only components (declared in the header)
Ideal for DTOs, value objects, and replacing verbose data classes — available since Java 16

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Custom Exceptions in Java
questions.question: How do you create custom exceptions in Java? When should you create checked vs unchecked custom exceptions?
questions.answer: Custom exceptions are user-defined exception classes that extend either Exception (for checked) or RuntimeException (for unchecked). You create them when Java's built-in exceptions don't adequately describe your domain-specific error condition. Creating a custom exception: extend the appropriate base class, provide constructors (at minimum: no-arg, message-only, message + cause), optionally add custom fields for additional error context. When to use checked custom exceptions: when the caller is expected to handle or recover from the error (e.g., InsufficientFundsException — the caller should handle it). When to use unchecked custom exceptions: for programming errors or invalid states that indicate a bug (e.g., InvalidUserStateException), or when forcing callers to handle exceptions would clutter the code (common in modern frameworks like Spring). Best practices: name exceptions clearly with the 'Exception' suffix, include the cause when wrapping other exceptions (exception chaining), provide meaningful messages, don't catch and ignore exceptions.
questions.explanation: Custom exceptions are like creating specialized alarm codes for a security system. Instead of a generic 'ALARM' signal, you have specific codes: 'UNAUTHORIZED_ACCESS', 'INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS', 'ACCOUNT_LOCKED'. This specificity lets the system (and developer) immediately understand what went wrong and respond appropriately, rather than guessing from a generic message.
key_takeaways: Extend Exception for checked custom exceptions; extend RuntimeException for unchecked ones
Always provide constructors: no-arg, message-only, and message + cause (for exception chaining)
Custom fields can carry domain-specific error context (e.g., amount, accountId)
Use checked exceptions for recoverable conditions the caller is expected to handle
Use unchecked exceptions for programming errors or when callers cannot reasonably recover
Exception chaining (cause parameter) preserves the root cause — always pass cause when wrapping
Name exceptions descriptively with 'Exception' suffix (InsufficientFundsException, not FundsError)

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: CompletableFuture in Java
questions.question: What is CompletableFuture in Java? How does it enable asynchronous, non-blocking programming?
questions.answer: CompletableFuture (introduced in Java 8) is an enhancement over Future that enables composable, non-blocking asynchronous programming. Regular Future has limitations: get() blocks the calling thread; no way to chain operations; no exception handling; cannot combine multiple Futures. CompletableFuture solves all of this. Key factory methods: supplyAsync(Supplier) — run a task in a thread pool and return a result; runAsync(Runnable) — run a task with no return value. Key chaining methods: thenApply(Function) — transform result (like Stream's map); thenAccept(Consumer) — consume result, no return; thenRun(Runnable) — run action after completion; thenCompose(Function returning CF) — chain async tasks (like flatMap); thenCombine(CF, BiFunction) — combine two independent CFs. Exception handling: exceptionally(Function) — handle exception, provide fallback; handle(BiFunction) — handles both result and exception. Async variants (thenApplyAsync) run on a separate thread from the common ForkJoinPool.
questions.explanation: Regular Future is like ordering takeout and then standing at the door staring until it arrives (blocking get()). CompletableFuture is like ordering food and then going about your day — when it arrives, you get a notification and can take a next action (like eating, or calling friends to join). You can even combine two orders: 'when pizza AND drinks arrive, serve dinner' (thenCombine).
key_takeaways: CompletableFuture enables non-blocking async programming — chain operations without waiting
supplyAsync() starts a computation in the ForkJoinPool; returns a CompletableFuture
thenApply() transforms the result (like map); thenAccept() consumes without returning
thenCompose() chains dependent async tasks (like flatMap for CFs)
thenCombine() combines two independent CompletableFutures when both complete
exceptionally() handles errors and provides a fallback value
allOf() waits for multiple CFs to all complete; anyOf() completes when the fastest finishes

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Sealed Classes and Pattern Matching
questions.question: What are sealed classes in Java? How do they work with pattern matching and switch expressions?
questions.answer: Sealed classes (Java 17+, preview since Java 15) restrict which classes can extend or implement them. By declaring a class or interface as sealed, you explicitly list all permitted subtypes using the permits clause. Each permitted subtype must be in the same package (or module) and must be declared as either: final (cannot be extended further), sealed (restricted extension), or non-sealed (open to extension by anyone). This creates a closed type hierarchy — the compiler knows all possible subtypes, enabling exhaustive pattern matching. Pattern Matching for switch (Java 21 final, preview since Java 16): allows matching an object against types in a switch expression, binding it to a typed variable in each case. When the switch is over a sealed type, the compiler can verify that all cases are covered (exhaustiveness checking) — no default needed if all permitted types are handled. Pattern matching for instanceof (Java 16) also reduces boilerplate: instead of 'if (obj instanceof String) { String s = (String) obj; }', write 'if (obj instanceof String s) { }'.
questions.explanation: Sealed classes are like a company policy that says 'our shapes can ONLY be Circle, Rectangle, or Triangle — no other shapes allowed.' This closed set of options lets the compiler guarantee that when you write a switch over a Shape, you must handle every possible kind. It's like a checklist the compiler enforces — you can't forget a case.
key_takeaways: Sealed classes restrict which subclasses are permitted — creates a closed, controlled hierarchy
Each permitted subtype must be final, sealed, or non-sealed
Sealed types enable exhaustive pattern matching in switch — compiler verifies all cases are covered
Pattern matching switch (Java 21): case Circle c -> ... binds matched type to variable c
Guarded patterns (when clause): case Circle c when c.radius > 10 -> for conditional matching
Pattern matching instanceof (Java 16): if (obj instanceof String s) — no explicit cast needed
Sealed types + pattern matching replace the need for visitor pattern and type-check chains

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: TreeMap and NavigableMap
questions.question: How does TreeMap work internally in Java? What are the NavigableMap methods that make it powerful?
questions.answer: TreeMap implements the NavigableMap interface and is backed by a Red-Black Tree — a self-balancing binary search tree. All keys are maintained in sorted order at all times, either in natural order (keys must implement Comparable) or using a provided Comparator. All basic operations (put, get, remove, containsKey) are O(log n). Because keys are sorted, TreeMap provides powerful navigation methods not available in HashMap: (1) firstKey() / lastKey() — get smallest/largest key. (2) floorKey(k) — greatest key ≤ k. (3) ceilingKey(k) — smallest key ≥ k. (4) lowerKey(k) — greatest key strictly < k. (5) higherKey(k) — smallest key strictly > k. (6) subMap(fromKey, toKey) — returns a view of keys in the range [from, to). (7) headMap(toKey) — keys strictly less than toKey. (8) tailMap(fromKey) — keys ≥ fromKey. (9) descendingMap() — reversed view. TreeMap does not allow null keys (because null cannot be compared) but allows null values.
questions.explanation: TreeMap is like a sorted filing cabinet with an intelligent index. Unlike HashMap (which is like a messy drawer you can only look up by exact label), TreeMap keeps everything alphabetically sorted. You can ask: 'give me all files between A and M,' 'what's the nearest file to N,' or 'what's the last file?' These navigational queries are what make TreeMap invaluable for range-based lookups.
key_takeaways: TreeMap is backed by a Red-Black Tree — O(log n) for all operations, always sorted by key
Keys must implement Comparable, or provide a Comparator — null keys not allowed
NavigableMap methods enable range queries: subMap(), headMap(), tailMap()
Floor/ceiling/lower/higher methods find nearest keys — powerful for interval lookups
descendingMap() and descendingKeySet() give reversed views without copying
Use TreeMap when you need sorted order or range-based key navigation
For sorted insertion order (not natural sort), use LinkedHashMap; for max performance, use HashMap

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: JIT Compilation and JVM Performance
questions.question: How does Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation work in the JVM? What are the key optimization techniques it applies?
questions.answer: The JVM executes Java bytecode using a combination of interpretation and JIT compilation. Initially, code is interpreted (slow, line by line). The JVM profiles running code to identify 'hot spots' — methods or loops executed frequently. These are compiled by the JIT compiler to native machine code for direct CPU execution. Java uses a tiered compilation system (Java 7+): Tier 0 — Interpreter; Tier 1-2 — C1 compiler (client compiler, fast compilation with light optimizations); Tier 3-4 — C2 compiler (server compiler, aggressive optimizations). Key JIT optimizations: (1) Inlining — replaces method calls with the method body, eliminating call overhead. (2) Dead Code Elimination — removes code that never executes. (3) Loop Unrolling — reduces loop overhead by replicating the loop body. (4) Escape Analysis — if an object doesn't escape the method, allocate it on the stack instead of heap (avoids GC pressure). (5) Lock Elision — if an object can't escape (can't be accessed by another thread), locks are removed. (6) Constant Folding — evaluates constant expressions at compile time. GraalVM's Ahead-Of-Time (AOT) compilation compiles Java to native binaries, eliminating JVM startup time.
questions.explanation: JIT is like a student who starts a course reading from notes slowly (interpreter). Over time, they notice they keep looking up the same formulas (hot code), so they memorize them (JIT compiles to native code). Now they can solve those problems instantly without looking anything up. The JVM does this automatically, identifying what's used most and making it as fast as possible.
key_takeaways: JIT compiles hot bytecode to native machine code at runtime — combining interpretation flexibility with native speed
Tiered compilation: Interpreter → C1 (fast, light optimizations) → C2 (slow, heavy optimizations)
Inlining: most important JIT optimization — replaces method calls with method body, unlocking further optimizations
Escape analysis: if object doesn't escape method scope, JIT allocates it on stack instead of heap
Lock elision: locks on thread-local objects are removed by JIT after escape analysis
Warm-up time: JVM needs some execution before JIT kicks in — cold starts are slower than hot runs
GraalVM AOT compilation eliminates JVM warm-up by producing native binaries from Java code

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Stream.of vs Stream.iterate vs Stream.generate
questions.question: What are the differences between Stream.of(), Stream.iterate(), and Stream.generate()? When should you use each?
questions.answer: These three are static factory methods for creating Streams, each suited to different use cases. (1) Stream.of(T... values): Creates a finite stream from explicit values or an array. Simple, most common for known collections of elements. (2) Stream.generate(Supplier<T>): Creates an infinite stream by calling a Supplier repeatedly. Each call to the Supplier produces the next element — ideal for random values, constants, or stateless generators. Since it's infinite, must be limited with limit(). The Supplier carries no state between calls. (3) Stream.iterate(seed, UnaryOperator) [Java 8]: Creates an infinite sequential stream starting from a seed and applying a function repeatedly. seed, f(seed), f(f(seed)), ... Carries state (the previous value). Must be limited. Java 9 overload: Stream.iterate(seed, Predicate, UnaryOperator) — terminates when the predicate returns false. This is like a while-loop form and is the preferred way to generate finite sequences programmatically.
questions.explanation: Stream.of() is like laying out specific cards on a table — you know exactly what's there. Stream.generate() is like rolling a dice over and over — each roll is independent, and you decide when to stop. Stream.iterate() is like counting steps — each step builds on the previous one (0, 1, 2, 3...), and you stop when you choose or when a condition is met.
key_takeaways: Stream.of(): finite stream from known elements — simplest and most common
Stream.generate(Supplier): infinite stateless stream — each element produced independently
Stream.iterate(seed, fn): infinite stateful stream — each element derived from the previous
Java 9 Stream.iterate(seed, predicate, fn): finite iterate with termination condition — like a for-loop
Stream.generate() and Stream.iterate() require limit() (or the Java 9 predicate) to avoid infinite loops
IntStream.range(start, end) / rangeClosed() are efficient for integer sequences without boxing
Use generate() for random/constant streams; iterate() for sequences where each value depends on the prior

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: ReentrantLock vs synchronized
questions.question: What is ReentrantLock in Java? How does it differ from synchronized and when should you prefer it?
questions.answer: ReentrantLock (java.util.concurrent.locks) is an explicit lock implementation that provides more advanced features than the synchronized keyword. Both provide mutual exclusion and memory visibility (happens-before), but ReentrantLock offers significantly more control. Key differences and advantages of ReentrantLock: (1) Interruptible lock acquisition: lockInterruptibly() — thread can be interrupted while waiting for the lock, preventing deadlocks. (2) Timed lock attempts: tryLock(timeout, unit) — tries to acquire lock for a time period, then gives up — enables lock timeout to avoid deadlock. (3) Fairness: new ReentrantLock(true) — guarantees longest-waiting thread gets the lock next (fair lock). synchronized is non-fair. (4) Multiple conditions: lock.newCondition() — allows multiple Condition objects per lock (more fine-grained than Object.wait()/notify()). (5) Lock inspection: isLocked(), getQueueLength(), isHeldByCurrentThread(). The 'reentrant' part: same as synchronized — the same thread can acquire the lock multiple times (reentrancy) without deadlocking, but must release it the same number of times. ALWAYS release the lock in a finally block.
questions.explanation: synchronized is like a basic hotel room key — insert, use the room, remove. ReentrantLock is like a hotel key card system with advanced features: you can set an expiry (tryLock timeout), cancel your booking if you wait too long (lockInterruptibly), guarantee fairness in a queue, and have multiple room sections (Conditions). More powerful, but you must remember to check out (unlock in finally).
key_takeaways: ReentrantLock provides mutual exclusion like synchronized but with advanced capabilities
tryLock(timeout): attempt to acquire lock for limited time — avoids permanent blocking (deadlock prevention)
lockInterruptibly(): waiting thread can be interrupted — enables thread cancellation while waiting
Fair lock (new ReentrantLock(true)): longest-waiting thread gets priority — prevents starvation
Multiple Conditions: lock.newCondition() allows fine-grained await/signal — more flexible than wait/notify
ALWAYS release in finally — failing to unlock causes permanent deadlock
Prefer synchronized for simple cases (less code, no risk of forgotten unlock); use ReentrantLock for advanced scenarios

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Java Module System (JPMS)
questions.question: What is the Java Module System (JPMS) introduced in Java 9? What problems does it solve and how does it work?
questions.answer: The Java Platform Module System (JPMS), also known as Project Jigsaw (Java 9), adds a higher-level unit of abstraction above packages: the module. Before Java 9, the JVM's classpath was a flat, unstructured list of JARs with no encapsulation at the package level — any public type was accessible from anywhere. Problems this caused: JAR hell (version conflicts, missing JARs only discovered at runtime), no strong encapsulation (internal implementation details accessible to anyone), and a monolithic JDK (every program loaded the entire JDK even if only a fraction was needed). JPMS introduces module-info.java — a module descriptor placed at the source root: module com.example.app { requires java.sql; exports com.example.api; } Key directives: requires (declares dependency on another module), exports (makes a package accessible to other modules), opens (allows reflective access), uses/provides (service loader integration). Benefits: strong encapsulation (unexported packages truly invisible), reliable configuration (dependencies verified at startup, not runtime), improved performance (JVM only loads needed platform modules), smaller deployments (jlink creates custom JREs with only required modules).
questions.explanation: Before JPMS, Java packages were like rooms with transparent walls — anyone could see and access anything public. JPMS adds solid walls with controlled doors. modules are like buildings (modules), and you explicitly decide which doors (exports) are open to the outside world and which other buildings (requires) you depend on. Missing a dependency is caught when you enter the building (at startup), not when you try to use a room (runtime crash).
key_takeaways: JPMS (Java 9) adds modules — a higher abstraction above packages with explicit dependency declaration
module-info.java: declares requires (dependencies) and exports (visible packages)
Strong encapsulation: unexported packages are truly invisible to other modules — even via reflection (unless opened)
Reliable configuration: missing module dependencies are reported at startup, not at runtime
exports ... to: restricted exports — only specific modules can access the package
opens: allows reflective access to a package — needed for frameworks (Spring, Hibernate, JUnit)
jlink: tool to create custom, minimal JREs containing only the modules your application needs

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Primitive Types vs Wrapper Classes
questions.question: What is the difference between primitive types and wrapper classes in Java?
questions.answer: Java has 8 primitive types: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, and boolean. Primitives store values directly in memory (stack), are not objects, and cannot be null. Wrapper classes (Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double, Character, Boolean) are objects that wrap a primitive value. They live on the heap and can be null. Wrapper classes provide utility methods (Integer.parseInt(), Double.parseDouble(), Integer.MAX_VALUE, etc.) and allow primitives to be used in collections like ArrayList<Integer> since generics require object types. Autoboxing/unboxing handles automatic conversion between primitives and wrappers. Key difference: primitives are faster and use less memory; wrappers are required when objects are needed (generics, null handling, utility methods).
questions.explanation: Think of a primitive as a raw coin and a wrapper class as the coin inside a plastic sleeve (object). The coin (value) is the same, but the sleeve lets you label it, store it in a collection designed for plastic-sleeved items, and it can be 'missing' (null — an empty sleeve). The raw coin is faster to handle, but you need the sleeve to work with more advanced systems.
key_takeaways: 8 primitive types: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean — stored directly on stack
Wrapper classes (Integer, Double, etc.) are objects on the heap — can be null
Wrapper classes provide utility methods: parseInt(), MAX_VALUE, toBinaryString(), etc.
Primitives are faster and use less memory than wrapper objects
Generics require wrapper types — ArrayList<Integer>, not ArrayList<int>
Autoboxing and unboxing handle automatic conversion between the two
Unboxing a null wrapper throws NullPointerException — always check for null

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: this and super Keywords
questions.question: What do the this and super keywords mean in Java? Where and how are they used?
questions.answer: this is a reference to the current object (the instance on which the method or constructor is called). Uses of this: (1) Distinguish instance variables from parameters with the same name: this.name = name. (2) Call another constructor in the same class: this(args) — must be the first statement. (3) Pass the current object as an argument. (4) Return the current object from a method (common in builder patterns). super is a reference to the parent class. Uses of super: (1) Access parent class fields or methods hidden or overridden by the child: super.methodName(). (2) Call the parent class constructor: super(args) — must be the first statement in a child constructor. If you do not explicitly call super(), Java automatically inserts a call to the no-arg super constructor. Note: this() and super() cannot both be first statements in a constructor — choose one.
questions.explanation: this is like saying 'myself' in a conversation — when talking about your own properties or calling your own other constructor. super is like saying 'as my parent taught me' — accessing or calling the parent's version of something. Every child constructor secretly calls the parent's constructor first (via super()) to make sure the parent is properly initialized before the child adds its own setup.
key_takeaways: this: reference to the current object instance — used to distinguish fields from parameters
this(args): calls another constructor in the same class — must be the first line
super: reference to the parent class — access parent fields and methods
super(args): calls the parent constructor — must be the first line in child constructor
If no explicit super() call is made, Java inserts super() automatically (calls no-arg parent constructor)
this() and super() cannot both appear as the first statement — only one allowed
Returning 'this' from a method enables fluent method chaining (as seen in builders)

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Interfaces — Default and Static Methods
questions.question: What are default and static methods in Java interfaces (Java 8+)? Why were they introduced?
questions.answer: Before Java 8, interfaces could only have abstract methods (public abstract) and constants (public static final). This made evolving interfaces backward-incompatible — adding a new method broke all implementing classes. Java 8 introduced: Default methods: methods with a body declared with the default keyword inside an interface. They provide a default implementation that all implementing classes inherit automatically. If a class wants different behavior, it can override the default method. This allows adding new methods to interfaces without breaking existing implementations. Static methods: methods with a body declared with static inside an interface. They belong to the interface itself (not to instances or implementing classes), cannot be overridden, and must be called on the interface name directly (Validator.validate(...)). Java 9 added private methods in interfaces — used to avoid code duplication between default methods.
questions.explanation: Before default methods, adding a new method to an interface was like changing the rules of a signed contract — everyone who signed it (implemented it) would be in breach. Default methods let you add new clauses with a pre-filled default response, so existing signatories aren't affected unless they want to customize it. Static methods in interfaces are like utility functions attached to the interface's namespace — they're helpers that belong to the interface concept, not to any implementor.
key_takeaways: default methods: interface methods with a body — provide backward-compatible additions to interfaces
All implementing classes inherit default methods automatically, but can override them
static methods in interfaces belong to the interface itself — called via InterfaceName.method()
Static interface methods cannot be overridden or inherited by implementing classes
Java 8 default methods enabled the Streams API (Collection.stream(), List.forEach()) without breaking existing code
If a class implements two interfaces with the same default method signature, it must override it to resolve the conflict
Java 9 added private methods in interfaces for sharing code between default methods

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Creating Threads in Java
questions.question: What are the different ways to create and start a thread in Java?
questions.answer: There are three main ways to create a thread in Java: (1) Extend the Thread class: Create a class that extends Thread and override the run() method. Then create an object of that class and call start(). (2) Implement the Runnable interface: Create a class that implements Runnable and implement the run() method. Pass it to a Thread constructor, then call start(). This is preferred because Java doesn't support multiple inheritance — implementing Runnable keeps the class open to extend other classes. (3) Use a Lambda expression (Java 8+): Since Runnable is a functional interface, pass a lambda directly to the Thread constructor — cleanest and most concise. Important: always call start(), never run() directly. Calling run() executes it on the current thread (no new thread is created). Modern Java prefers using ExecutorService over raw Thread creation for better resource management.
questions.explanation: Creating a thread is like hiring a worker. Extending Thread is like creating a specialized subcontractor who can only do one job type (single inheritance limit). Implementing Runnable is like hiring a generic contractor and giving them a task list (Runnable) — the contractor (Thread) can potentially do other things too. Lambda is like texting the task to a temp worker — the most concise and modern approach.
key_takeaways: Three ways: extend Thread, implement Runnable, or use a lambda expression
Always call start() to create a new OS thread — calling run() executes on the current thread
Implementing Runnable is preferred over extending Thread — keeps inheritance open
Lambda works because Runnable is a @FunctionalInterface with one abstract method (run())
Thread.currentThread().getName() gives the executing thread's name
join() waits for a thread to complete before continuing in the calling thread
For production code, prefer ExecutorService over raw Thread creation for better lifecycle management

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Introduction to Java Streams
questions.question: What is the Java Stream API? How do you use filter(), map(), and collect() together?
questions.answer: The Java Stream API (introduced in Java 8) provides a functional, declarative way to process sequences of data. A Stream is not a data structure — it is a pipeline of operations applied to a data source (like a Collection or array). Key concepts: Stream operations are either intermediate (return another Stream, lazy) or terminal (produce a result, trigger execution). filter(Predicate) — keeps only elements that match the condition; returns a Stream. map(Function) — transforms each element to another value; returns a Stream. collect(Collector) — a terminal operation that gathers elements into a collection (List, Set, Map, etc.). A typical stream pipeline is: source.stream().filter(...).map(...).collect(...). Streams are single-use — once a terminal operation is called, the stream is consumed and cannot be reused.
questions.explanation: Think of a Stream as a conveyor belt at a factory. The raw parts (data source) enter one end. filter() is a worker who removes defective parts. map() is a worker who paints or reshapes each part. collect() is the box at the end that collects the finished parts. The belt only moves when the collect() box is placed at the end — that's lazy evaluation.
key_takeaways: Stream is a pipeline of operations on a data source — not a data structure itself
filter(Predicate): keeps elements matching the condition (intermediate, lazy)
map(Function): transforms each element to another value (intermediate, lazy)
collect(Collector): terminal operation that triggers execution and gathers results
Streams are single-use — a consumed stream cannot be reused
Method references (String::toUpperCase, System.out::println) are shorthand for certain lambdas
Use Collectors.toList(), toSet(), toMap(), joining() to accumulate results

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: String Pool and String Interning
questions.question: What is the Java String Pool? How does intern() work and what are the memory implications?
questions.answer: The String Pool (also called String Constant Pool) is a special region of the JVM Heap (part of Metaspace since Java 8) that stores unique String literals. When you write String s = "hello", Java first checks the pool. If "hello" already exists, the same reference is returned — no new object is created. This is why two string literals with the same content share the same reference (== returns true). When you use new String("hello"), a new object is always created on the regular heap outside the pool — even if "hello" already exists in the pool. String.intern(): If called on a String object, it checks the pool for an equal String. If found, returns the pool reference. If not, adds the string to the pool and returns the pool reference. This allows == comparison to work between interned strings. Memory implication: The pool reduces memory usage for commonly repeated strings, but interning thousands of dynamic strings can fill up the pool and increase GC pressure.
questions.explanation: The String Pool is like a shared library. When everyone needs the same book (String literal), instead of printing a new copy for each person, the library gives them all a reference to the same copy on the shelf. new String() is like printing your own personal copy even when the library already has one. intern() is like checking your personal copy into the library — if the library already has it, they give you back the library's copy and discard yours.
key_takeaways: String Pool is a cache of unique String literals in the JVM Heap — reuses objects for identical literals
String literals ("hello") automatically go into the pool; new String("hello") bypasses it
Two string literals with the same content share the same pool reference — == returns true
intern() returns the canonical pool reference; allows == comparison for interned strings
Compile-time constant expressions ("a" + "b") are pooled; runtime concatenation is not
Always use .equals() to compare String content — never rely on == for string equality
The pool reduces memory usage for repeated strings but don't intern large volumes of dynamic strings

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Deque and ArrayDeque
questions.question: What is a Deque in Java? How is ArrayDeque used as both a Stack and Queue?
questions.answer: Deque (Double-Ended Queue, pronounced 'deck') is an interface in java.util that extends Queue and allows elements to be added or removed from both ends — front and back — efficiently. ArrayDeque is the most commonly used Deque implementation, backed by a resizable circular array. Key operations: addFirst() / offerFirst() — add to front; addLast() / offerLast() — add to back; removeFirst() / pollFirst() — remove from front; removeLast() / pollLast() — remove from back; peekFirst() / peekLast() — view without removing. ArrayDeque as Stack: push() = addFirst(), pop() = removeFirst(), peek() = peekFirst(). ArrayDeque as Queue: offer() = offerLast(), poll() = pollFirst(). ArrayDeque is faster than both the legacy Stack class (which extends Vector and is synchronized) and LinkedList for stack/queue operations because it avoids synchronization and node allocation overhead. Does not allow null elements.
questions.explanation: A Deque is like a line of people that can be managed from both ends — you can add or remove from the front or the back. ArrayDeque is the most practical implementation because it uses a circular array (efficient resizing) without synchronization overhead. Use it as a stack (LIFO) by working only with the front, or as a queue (FIFO) by adding to the back and removing from the front.
key_takeaways: Deque: double-ended queue — supports O(1) add/remove from both front and back
ArrayDeque is backed by a resizable circular array — no synchronization, no node overhead
As Stack: push()/pop()/peek() operate on the front (LIFO)
As Queue: offer()/poll()/peek() — offer adds to back, poll removes from front (FIFO)
ArrayDeque is faster than Stack (no synchronization) and LinkedList (no node allocation)
ArrayDeque does not allow null elements
Prefer ArrayDeque over the legacy Stack class for all stack use cases

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Composition vs Inheritance
questions.question: What is the difference between composition and inheritance in Java? When should you use each?
questions.answer: Inheritance is an 'is-a' relationship — a Dog IS-A Animal. A child class extends the parent class, inheriting its state and behavior. Composition is a 'has-a' relationship — a Car HAS-A Engine. A class holds a reference to another class as a field, delegating behavior to it. The 'Favor Composition Over Inheritance' principle (from Effective Java and GoF) suggests that composition often provides more flexible and maintainable designs: (1) Loose coupling — the containing class doesn't depend on the internal implementation of the contained class. (2) Flexibility at runtime — you can swap the composed object. (3) Avoids fragile base class problem — changes to the parent class unexpectedly break child classes. Use inheritance when: a true 'is-a' relationship exists and you intend to use the child polymorphically as the parent. Use composition when: you want to reuse code but there's no genuine 'is-a' relationship, or the hierarchy would become too deep or fragile.
questions.explanation: Inheritance is like being born into a family — you automatically inherit traits. Composition is like assembling a robot — you pick the best parts from different manufacturers and combine them. If the microphone part breaks, you replace just that part; you don't have to redesign the whole robot. Inheritance is tighter and harder to change; composition is modular and flexible.
key_takeaways: Inheritance: 'is-a' relationship — subclass is a specialized version of the parent
Composition: 'has-a' relationship — class contains an instance of another as a field
Favor composition over inheritance — more flexible, loosely coupled, and easier to change
Inheritance can cause the 'fragile base class' problem — parent changes break child unexpectedly
Composition allows runtime swapping of behavior (dependency injection uses this principle)
Use inheritance when a genuine 'is-a' relationship exists AND polymorphism is needed
Deep inheritance hierarchies become hard to understand and maintain — prefer shallow hierarchies

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Multi-catch and Exception Hierarchy
questions.question: How does Java's exception hierarchy work? What is multi-catch and when should you use it?
questions.answer: Java's exception hierarchy starts at Throwable (the root). It has two main branches: Error (serious JVM problems like OutOfMemoryError — do not catch) and Exception. Exception branches into: checked exceptions (IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException — must be handled or declared) and RuntimeException (unchecked: NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException — compiler does not require handling). Multi-catch (Java 7+) allows a single catch block to handle multiple unrelated exception types, separated by the pipe character |. This eliminates duplicated catch blocks when the same handling logic applies to multiple exceptions. Rules: the exceptions in a multi-catch must be unrelated (one cannot be a subtype of the other). The caught variable in multi-catch is implicitly final. When catching multiple exceptions in separate blocks, always order from most specific to most general — catching Exception before IOException will never reach the IOException block (compile error).
questions.explanation: The exception hierarchy is like a family tree of failures. Throwable is the great-grandparent. Error is the 'catastrophic illness' branch (don't try to treat). Exception is the 'treatable illness' branch — checked ones are chronic conditions the doctor (compiler) makes you prepare for, unchecked ones are sudden accidents. Multi-catch is like having one treatment protocol for a group of related symptoms — instead of writing the same protocol three times for fever, headache, and fatigue, you write it once for all three.
key_takeaways: Exception hierarchy: Throwable → Error (don't catch) / Exception (handle these)
Checked exceptions extend Exception; unchecked extend RuntimeException
Multi-catch (Java 7+): catch (IOException | SQLException e) handles multiple exceptions in one block
Multi-catch exceptions must be unrelated — one cannot be a subclass of the other
The caught variable in multi-catch is implicitly final — cannot be reassigned
Order catch blocks from most specific to most general — broader catches must come last
Catching a parent exception type (Exception) before a subtype (FileNotFoundException) causes a compile error

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Collections Utility Class
questions.question: What useful methods does the java.util.Collections class provide?
questions.answer: java.util.Collections is a utility class containing exclusively static methods that operate on or return collections. Key methods: (1) sort(List) — sorts using natural order (Comparable); sort(List, Comparator) — sorts with a custom comparator. (2) reverse(List) — reverses the order of elements. (3) shuffle(List) — randomly shuffles elements. (4) min(Collection) / max(Collection) — finds min/max using natural ordering. (5) frequency(Collection, Object) — counts occurrences of an element. (6) swap(List, i, j) — swaps elements at positions i and j. (7) fill(List, obj) — replaces all elements with a specified value. (8) copy(dest, src) — copies all elements from src to dest (dest must be at least as large). (9) unmodifiableList(list) / unmodifiableMap(map) — returns a read-only view (modifications throw UnsupportedOperationException). (10) synchronizedList(list) — returns a thread-safe wrapper. (11) nCopies(n, obj) — returns an immutable List with n copies of obj. (12) disjoint(c1, c2) — returns true if the two collections have no common elements.
questions.explanation: Collections is like a Swiss Army knife for collection types. Just as java.util.Arrays provides helpers for arrays, Collections provides helpers for any Collection or List. Think of it as outsourcing common operations — instead of writing a custom sort, reverse, or shuffle, you call the provided tool. Note: Java 8+ Streams and List.of() have made some of these less necessary, but Collections utility methods remain widely used.
key_takeaways: Collections is a utility class with static methods for operating on Collection objects
sort(), reverse(), shuffle() operate on Lists in-place
min() and max() work on any Collection with natural ordering or a Comparator
unmodifiableList/Map/Set returns a read-only view — add/remove throws UnsupportedOperationException
synchronizedList() wraps a list for thread safety (prefer CopyOnWriteArrayList or ConcurrentHashMap)
frequency() counts occurrences; disjoint() checks if two collections share no elements
Java 9+ List.of(), Map.of(), Set.of() provide factory methods for immutable collections (no need for Collections.unmodifiable...)

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Stack Memory vs Heap Memory
questions.question: What is the difference between Stack memory and Heap memory in the JVM?
questions.answer: The JVM divides runtime memory into two key areas: Stack and Heap. Stack Memory: Each thread has its own stack. Stores method call frames — each frame contains local variables, method parameters, and the return address. Stack memory is allocated and freed automatically as methods are called and return (LIFO). Access is very fast. Stack size is limited — too many nested calls causes StackOverflowError. Variables on the stack hold either primitive values directly or references (pointers) to heap objects. Heap Memory: Shared among all threads. Stores all objects created with new and instance variables. Objects live on the heap until they are garbage collected. Heap memory is divided into Young Generation and Old Generation for GC purposes. Running out of heap throws OutOfMemoryError. Key rule: primitives declared inside a method are on the stack; objects (even if referenced by a local variable) are on the heap. The reference variable is on the stack, the object it points to is on the heap.
questions.explanation: Think of Stack as your desk at work — small, fast, and personal to you. You keep your current papers (local variables) there. When you finish a task (method returns), you clear the desk. Heap is the shared filing room — large, shared, and managed by a filing clerk (Garbage Collector). When you need more space, you put a label on your desk (reference variable) that tells you where in the filing room (heap) the actual folder (object) is stored.
key_takeaways: Stack: per-thread, stores method frames with local variables and primitives — fast, limited size
Heap: shared across threads, stores all objects created with new — managed by GC
Local primitive variables are stored on the stack; objects are always on the heap
Reference variables (pointers to objects) are on the stack; the objects they point to are on the heap
StackOverflowError: too many nested method calls exhaust the stack
OutOfMemoryError: heap is full — not enough memory for new objects
Instance variables are stored on the heap as part of their object

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Immutability in Java
questions.question: What does it mean for a class to be immutable in Java? How do you design one correctly?
questions.answer: An immutable object is one whose state cannot be changed after construction. Once created, its values are fixed for its lifetime. Benefits: thread-safe by nature (no synchronization needed), safe to share and cache, easier to reason about, great as Map keys or Set elements. To create an immutable class: (1) Declare the class as final — prevents subclassing that could override methods to allow mutation. (2) Make all fields private and final — ensures they are assigned once and not accessible directly. (3) Do not provide setters or any methods that modify state. (4) Initialize all fields in the constructor. (5) If any field is a mutable object (like List or Date), create defensive copies in the constructor and in getters — never expose the mutable reference directly. Java's String, Integer, LocalDate, and all primitive wrapper classes are immutable. Java 14+ Records are also immutable by default.
questions.explanation: Immutability is like issuing a certificate — once printed and signed, its content never changes. If someone asks to see it, you give them a copy, not the original (defensive copying). The benefit is you can hand this certificate to thousands of people simultaneously without worrying anyone will tamper with it — that's why immutable objects are naturally thread-safe.
key_takeaways: Immutable object: state cannot change after construction — thread-safe, cacheable, safe as map keys
Rules: final class, private final fields, no setters, initialize all in constructor
Defensive copy: if a field is a mutable type (List, Date), copy it in the constructor AND in getters
Returning the raw mutable reference from a getter breaks immutability — the caller could modify it
String, Integer, LocalDate, all wrapper classes, and Records are immutable in Java
Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe — no synchronization required
Java 16+ Records automatically enforce immutability for their components

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: wait(), notify(), and notifyAll()
questions.question: What are wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() in Java? How are they used for thread communication?
questions.answer: wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() are methods defined on java.lang.Object used for inter-thread communication — allowing threads to coordinate access to shared resources. They must be called from within a synchronized block or method (otherwise IllegalMonitorStateException is thrown). wait(): suspends the current thread and releases the object's monitor lock, allowing other threads to acquire it. The thread remains suspended until another thread calls notify() or notifyAll() on the same object, or a timeout expires. notify(): wakes up one randomly chosen thread that is waiting on the object. The awakened thread must re-acquire the lock before continuing. notifyAll(): wakes up ALL threads waiting on the object. All must then compete to re-acquire the lock — only one will win at a time. Best practice: always call wait() inside a while loop (not if), because the thread should re-check the condition after being woken — spurious wakeups can occur where a thread wakes without being notified.
questions.explanation: Imagine a kitchen with one chef (the shared resource) and multiple waiters. wait() is a waiter stepping back and releasing the kitchen access, waiting to be called. notify() is someone calling one random waiter back in. notifyAll() calls all waiters back, but only one can use the kitchen at a time — they must re-check if the kitchen is still available (while loop condition check). This is the classic producer-consumer coordination pattern.
key_takeaways: wait(), notify(), notifyAll() must be called inside a synchronized block on the same object
wait(): releases the lock and suspends the calling thread until notified
notify(): wakes one randomly chosen waiting thread — it must re-acquire the lock before continuing
notifyAll(): wakes all waiting threads — all compete for the lock, only one proceeds at a time
Always use while loop (not if) around wait() to re-check condition after wakeup — spurious wakeups can occur
Calling wait()/notify() outside synchronized throws IllegalMonitorStateException
For modern thread communication, prefer java.util.concurrent alternatives: BlockingQueue, Condition, CountDownLatch

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Method References in Java
questions.question: What are method references in Java? Explain the four types with examples.
questions.answer: Method references (introduced in Java 8) are a shorthand syntax for lambda expressions that simply call an existing method. Instead of (args) -> ClassName.method(args), you write ClassName::method. They improve readability by reducing boilerplate. There are four types: (1) Static method reference: ClassName::staticMethod — replaces a lambda that calls a static method. Example: Integer::parseInt instead of s -> Integer.parseInt(s). (2) Instance method reference of a particular instance: instance::method — calls a method on a specific object. Example: System.out::println instead of s -> System.out.println(s). (3) Instance method reference of an arbitrary instance of a type: ClassName::instanceMethod — the first argument of the lambda becomes the target of the call. Example: String::toUpperCase instead of s -> s.toUpperCase(). (4) Constructor reference: ClassName::new — creates a new instance. Example: ArrayList::new instead of () -> new ArrayList<>(). Method references can only be used where a functional interface is expected.
questions.explanation: Method references are like speed-dialing. Instead of dialing a full number every time (writing out a lambda), you store it under one name and just press that button. They're not new functionality — they're syntax shortcuts for lambdas that call exactly one existing method. When you see ::, think 'I'm referring to this method as a function.'
key_takeaways: Method references are shorthand for lambdas that call exactly one existing method
Static method reference: ClassName::staticMethod (e.g., Integer::parseInt)
Instance method on a specific object: instance::method (e.g., System.out::println)
Instance method on an arbitrary object of a type: Type::instanceMethod (e.g., String::toUpperCase)
Constructor reference: ClassName::new (e.g., ArrayList::new)
Method references are only valid where a functional interface is expected
They improve readability by eliminating trivial lambda wrappers around existing methods

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Memory Leaks in Java
questions.question: Can Java have memory leaks? What are common causes and how do you detect and prevent them?
questions.answer: Yes, Java can have memory leaks even with automatic garbage collection. A memory leak in Java occurs when objects are no longer needed by the application but are still reachable (still have at least one live reference) — so the GC cannot collect them. Over time, these accumulate and eventually cause OutOfMemoryError. Common causes: (1) Static collections — objects added to a static Map/List/Set and never removed; the collection lives as long as the class. (2) Unclosed resources — connections, streams, or file handles not properly closed (prefer try-with-resources). (3) Listeners and callbacks not deregistered — event listeners registered but never removed when the listener object is done. (4) ThreadLocal not removed — a ThreadLocal variable in a thread pool thread is reused; if not cleaned with remove(), the value persists. (5) Inner class references — a non-static inner class holds an implicit reference to its outer class. (6) Caches without eviction — unbounded caches grow indefinitely. Detection tools: Java VisualVM, Eclipse MAT (Memory Analyzer Tool), JProfiler, heap dumps, and monitoring GC logs with -verbose:gc.
questions.explanation: Garbage collection only removes what it can reach — it's like a cleaning robot that ignores anything tied down. A memory leak is an accumulation of objects that are still 'tied down' (referenced) even though the application is done with them. A static map that collects user sessions but never removes them is like a hotel that never checks out guests — eventually, all rooms fill up and new guests (objects) can't get in.
key_takeaways: Java can have memory leaks — GC only collects unreachable objects; still-referenced unused objects are leaked
Common causes: static collections, unclosed resources, un-deregistered listeners, ThreadLocal in pools
Static collections that grow indefinitely are the most common source of leaks in server apps
Always use try-with-resources for closeable resources — prevents resource/handle leaks
Always call ThreadLocal.remove() in thread pool tasks — threads are reused, values persist between tasks
WeakHashMap and SoftReference allow GC to reclaim entries — useful for caches
Detect leaks with heap dumps, Eclipse MAT, VisualVM, or -verbose:gc JVM flags

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Enum in Java
questions.question: What are enums in Java? What advanced features do they support beyond simple constants?
questions.answer: An enum (enumeration) is a special class type in Java that represents a fixed set of named constants. Declared with the enum keyword. Enums implicitly extend java.lang.Enum and cannot extend any other class (but can implement interfaces). Beyond simple constants, Java enums support: (1) Fields and constructors — enum constants can have associated data (e.g., a planet's mass and radius). (2) Methods — enums can define and override methods per constant using anonymous class bodies. (3) Abstract methods — force each constant to provide its own implementation. (4) Implementing interfaces — enums can implement any number of interfaces. (5) Built-in methods: values() returns all constants as an array; valueOf(String) returns the constant by name; ordinal() returns the index; name() returns the name as a String. (6) EnumSet and EnumMap — highly efficient specialized collections designed for enum keys. Enums are thread-safe by design and are the safest Singleton pattern (immune to reflection and serialization attacks).
questions.explanation: Enums are like custom types with a restricted membership list. A basic enum is like a closed club with named members. Advanced enums are like those members each having their own profile (fields), capabilities (methods), and some members having special skills (abstract method overrides). The Planet enum is the classic example — each planet has its own mass and radius, and you can calculate surface gravity per planet.
key_takeaways: Enum is a special class with a fixed set of named constants — implicitly extends java.lang.Enum
Enums can have fields, constructors (always private), and methods
Abstract methods in enums force each constant to provide its own implementation
Built-in methods: values(), valueOf(), name(), ordinal(), compareTo()
Enums can implement interfaces but cannot extend any class (already extends Enum)
Enum is the safest Singleton — immune to reflection attacks and serialization issues
EnumSet and EnumMap are highly efficient specialized collections for enum-typed keys

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: BlockingQueue and Producer-Consumer Pattern
questions.question: What is BlockingQueue in Java? How does it simplify the Producer-Consumer pattern?
questions.answer: BlockingQueue (java.util.concurrent) is a thread-safe queue that supports blocking operations: put() blocks the calling thread if the queue is full (waits until space is available); take() blocks the calling thread if the queue is empty (waits until an element is available). This makes it perfect for the Producer-Consumer pattern without manual synchronization, wait(), or notify(). Main implementations: ArrayBlockingQueue — bounded, backed by a fixed-size array; LinkedBlockingQueue — optionally bounded, backed by linked nodes; PriorityBlockingQueue — unbounded, elements ordered by priority; SynchronousQueue — capacity of zero — each put() must wait for a corresponding take() (direct handoff). Key methods: put(e) / offer(e, timeout) — insert element (put blocks if full); take() / poll(timeout) — remove element (take blocks if empty); peek() — view without removing; remainingCapacity() — space available; size() — current count. Using BlockingQueue eliminates the need for explicit synchronization, wait/notify loops, and reduces the risk of bugs like missed signals and spurious wakeups.
questions.explanation: BlockingQueue is like a conveyor belt between workers. The producer puts parts on the belt — if it's full, the producer pauses and waits. The consumer takes parts from the belt — if it's empty, the consumer pauses and waits. Neither needs to explicitly coordinate or signal the other — the belt (BlockingQueue) handles all the blocking and signaling internally. This replaces manual synchronized + wait/notify with a clean, safe, and idiomatic solution.
key_takeaways: BlockingQueue: thread-safe queue where put() blocks if full and take() blocks if empty
Eliminates manual synchronized + wait/notify for Producer-Consumer — simpler, safer, bug-resistant
ArrayBlockingQueue: bounded (fixed capacity); LinkedBlockingQueue: optionally bounded; PriorityBlockingQueue: priority ordering
SynchronousQueue: zero-capacity — each put() directly hands off to a waiting take()
poll(timeout): non-indefinite blocking alternative to take() — returns null if no element arrives in time
offer(e, timeout): non-indefinite blocking alternative to put() — returns false if no space in time
Widely used in thread pools, task schedulers, and event-driven systems

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Annotations in Java
questions.question: What are annotations in Java? How do you create custom annotations and process them?
questions.answer: Annotations are metadata attached to code elements (classes, methods, fields, parameters, etc.) that provide information to the compiler, build tools, or runtime frameworks — without directly affecting the program logic. Syntax: @interface AnnotationName { elements... }. Built-in annotations: @Override (compiler check), @Deprecated (marks obsolete), @SuppressWarnings, @FunctionalInterface, @SafeVarargs. Meta-annotations configure annotations themselves: @Target (where the annotation can be applied — METHOD, FIELD, TYPE, etc.), @Retention (how long it's kept — SOURCE, CLASS, or RUNTIME), @Documented (include in Javadoc), @Inherited (subclasses inherit the annotation). To create a custom annotation: define it with @interface, configure with meta-annotations. To process annotations at runtime: use the Reflection API — getAnnotation(), isAnnotationPresent(). Annotation processors (javax.annotation.processing) process SOURCE or CLASS retention annotations at compile time — used heavily by frameworks like Lombok, MapStruct, and Dagger. Frameworks like Spring (@Component, @Autowired), JPA (@Entity, @Column), and JUnit (@Test) are largely built on annotations.
questions.explanation: Annotations are like sticky notes attached to code. Some sticky notes are for the compiler ('check this override'), some for the build tool ('generate code here'), and some are kept at runtime for frameworks to read ('treat this class as a Spring bean'). The @Retention controls how long the sticky note survives: SOURCE (thrown away after compilation), CLASS (stored in the .class file), RUNTIME (available for reflection while running).
key_takeaways: Annotations attach metadata to code — processed by compiler, build tools, or runtime frameworks
Define with @interface; configure with meta-annotations (@Target, @Retention, @Documented)
@Retention: SOURCE (compile only), CLASS (in .class file), RUNTIME (accessible via reflection)
@Target: controls where annotation can be applied (METHOD, FIELD, TYPE, PARAMETER, etc.)
Runtime annotations are read via Reflection API: getAnnotation(), isAnnotationPresent()
Compile-time annotation processors (APT) generate code at build time — used by Lombok, MapStruct, Dagger
Frameworks like Spring, JPA/Hibernate, and JUnit are largely built on annotations

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Custom Collectors in Java
questions.question: How do you create a custom Collector in Java Streams? What are the components of the Collector interface?
questions.answer: A Collector is the strategy used by the collect() terminal operation to accumulate stream elements into a result container. The Collector<T, A, R> interface has three type parameters: T — the element type; A — the mutable accumulation type (intermediate container); R — the final result type. Five components define a Collector: (1) supplier() — provides a new empty mutable result container (e.g., () -> new ArrayList<>()). (2) accumulator() — adds one element into the container (BiConsumer<A, T>). (3) combiner() — merges two partial results (BinaryOperator<A>) — used for parallel streams. (4) finisher() — optionally transforms the accumulation container into the final result (Function<A, R>). (5) characteristics() — a Set of Collector.Characteristics (CONCURRENT, UNORDERED, IDENTITY_FINISH) that help the stream pipeline optimize execution. Collector.of() provides a convenient factory method to build a custom Collector without implementing the interface directly. For most use cases, Collectors provides all necessary implementations — custom collectors are only needed for truly unique accumulation logic.
questions.explanation: A custom Collector is like designing a custom sorting machine. The supplier() builds the empty machine. The accumulator() defines how each incoming item is processed into the machine. The combiner() explains how to merge two partial machines (when running in parallel). The finisher() finalizes the output — maybe packs items into a box. Most of the time, you'll use the standard machines (Collectors.toList() etc.), but when you need a unique output format, you build your own.
key_takeaways: Collector<T, A, R>: T=element type, A=mutable accumulation container, R=final result
Five components: supplier(), accumulator(), combiner(), finisher(), characteristics()
Collector.of() is the convenient factory for creating custom collectors without implementing the interface
combiner() is only used for parallel streams — merges partial results from different threads
IDENTITY_FINISH characteristic: skips the finisher when A == R (no transformation needed)
UNORDERED characteristic: stream can process elements in any order — enables optimizations
Build custom collectors only for logic not covered by built-in Collectors; standard Collectors cover most needs

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Java Virtual Threads (Project Loom)
questions.question: What are Virtual Threads in Java (Project Loom)? How do they differ from platform threads and what problems do they solve?
questions.answer: Virtual Threads (Java 21, preview since Java 19) are lightweight threads managed by the JVM, not the operating system. Traditional Java threads (Platform Threads) map 1:1 to OS threads — expensive to create (typically 1MB+ stack), limited to thousands per JVM, and block an OS thread when waiting on I/O. Virtual Threads are multiplexed over a small pool of carrier (OS) threads by the JVM's scheduler. A JVM can run millions of virtual threads simultaneously. Key behavior: when a virtual thread blocks (on I/O, sleep, synchronized, etc.), it is unmounted from the carrier thread — the carrier thread is freed to run other virtual threads. When the blocking operation completes, the virtual thread is remounted. Creation: Thread.ofVirtual().start(runnable) or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor(). Virtual threads excel at high-throughput I/O-bound workloads (web servers, microservices). They do NOT improve CPU-bound work. The main benefit: you can write simple synchronous-looking code that scales like asynchronous code — without reactive frameworks like CompletableFuture chaining. Pinning: if virtual thread code calls a synchronized block or native method, it may be 'pinned' to the carrier thread — prefer ReentrantLock over synchronized in virtual thread-heavy code.
questions.explanation: Platform threads are like expensive full-time employees — each costs significant resources (OS thread + stack). Virtual threads are like task cards handled by a small team of workers (carrier threads). When a task card is waiting for something (I/O), the worker puts it aside and picks up another task card. When the waiting is done, any available worker picks up that card and continues. This lets a tiny team handle millions of task cards concurrently — much more efficient for waiting-heavy workloads.
key_takeaways: Virtual Threads are JVM-managed lightweight threads — not OS threads; millions can exist simultaneously
When a virtual thread blocks (I/O, sleep), it unmounts from the carrier OS thread — frees CPU for others
Platform threads: 1:1 with OS threads, ~1MB stack, limited to thousands; Virtual threads: JVM-managed, kilobytes
Create with Thread.ofVirtual().start() or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()
Best for high-throughput I/O-bound workloads (web servers, DB calls, network I/O)
Do NOT help with CPU-bound work — no more CPU time is created; only concurrency improves
Avoid synchronized on objects in virtual threads — prefer ReentrantLock to prevent carrier thread pinning

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Varargs in Java
questions.question: What are varargs (variable arguments) in Java and how do they work?
questions.answer: Varargs let a method accept zero or more arguments of a specified type without forcing the caller to wrap them in an array and without you writing multiple overloaded methods for different argument counts. The syntax is type... paramName, and it must be the last parameter in the method signature — a method can have only one varargs parameter. Internally, the compiler treats the varargs parameter as an array: when you call the method, Java automatically packages the passed arguments into an array (or you can pass an actual array directly instead of individual values). Calling the method with zero arguments creates an empty array, never null. During overload resolution, Java always prefers an exact-match (non-varargs) method over a varargs method if both are applicable, so varargs methods are only chosen when no better match exists. One subtlety: using varargs with generic types (e.g., List<T>... lists) can trigger 'unchecked generic array creation' warnings because arrays and generics don't mix safely (heap pollution) — if you're certain the usage is safe, you can suppress this with @SafeVarargs on the method.
questions.explanation: Think of varargs like a flexible drive-through order — instead of needing a separate menu line for 'order of 1 item', 'order of 2 items', 'order of 3 items', you just say 'I'll take these' and hand over however many items you want, and the kitchen (the method) automatically treats them as a single order list.
key_takeaways: Varargs are declared as type... paramName and must be the last parameter in the method signature
Internally compiled as an array — you can pass individual values, zero values, or an actual array directly
Calling with zero arguments produces an empty array, never null
Only one varargs parameter is allowed per method, and it must come last
Overload resolution always prefers an exact-match method over a varargs method when both apply
Generic varargs can cause heap pollution warnings — suppress with @SafeVarargs only when the usage is verified safe

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: hashCode() and equals() Contract
questions.question: What is the contract between hashCode() and equals() in Java, and why must they be overridden together?
questions.answer: Java's general contract states: (1) if two objects are equal according to equals(), they MUST return the same hashCode(); (2) if two objects return the same hashCode(), they are NOT required to be equal (hash collisions are legal and expected); (3) hashCode() must be consistent — calling it multiple times on the same unchanged object must always return the same value within a single execution. Hash-based collections like HashMap, HashSet, and HashTable rely entirely on this contract: they first use hashCode() to determine which internal bucket an object belongs to, and only then use equals() to check for an exact match within that bucket (to resolve collisions). If you override equals() to compare logical content but forget to override hashCode(), two 'equal' objects can end up with different default hash codes (based on memory address) and therefore land in different buckets — meaning a HashSet might store duplicates it thinks are different, or a HashMap.get() might fail to find a value even though an 'equal' key was used to insert it. The safe approach is to always override both together, basing both on the same set of meaningful fields, and to use Objects.equals() and Objects.hash() (or generated code) to avoid mistakes — and ideally only include fields that don't change after the object is used as a hash key, since mutating a field used in hashCode() after insertion can make the object unfindable in the collection.
questions.explanation: Think of hashCode() as the row number in a giant warehouse and equals() as checking the label on the specific box once you reach that row. If two boxes have identical labels (equals() is true) but the warehouse sends you to two different rows (different hashCode()), you'll never find the second box by looking for the first — the system breaks down. Always make sure that 'equal content' always sends you to the same row.
key_takeaways: Equal objects (per equals()) MUST produce equal hashCode() values - this is mandatory
Equal hashCode() values do NOT guarantee the objects are equal - collisions are normal and expected
HashMap/HashSet use hashCode() to pick a bucket first, then equals() to confirm a match within that bucket
Overriding equals() without hashCode() breaks hash-based collections - duplicates won't be detected and lookups can silently fail
Use Objects.hash(fields...) and Objects.equals() to implement both correctly and consistently
Avoid using mutable fields in hashCode() if the object will be used as a key in a hash-based collection

questions.subject: Java
questions.title: Text Blocks in Java
questions.question: What are text blocks in Java, and how do they make multi-line strings easier to write?
questions.answer: Text blocks (standardized in Java 15, previewed in 13 and 14) let you write multi-line string literals using triple double-quotes (""") instead of concatenating strings line by line with \n and escaping every embedded quote. The opening """ must be followed by a line terminator, and the content's indentation is automatically normalized: Java strips the 'incidental' leading whitespace common to all lines based on the indentation of the closing delimiter, while preserving the meaningful relative indentation of the content itself. A trailing backslash (\) at the end of a line suppresses the implicit newline that would otherwise be inserted, letting you wrap long lines in source code without adding a literal line break in the resulting string. The sequence \s can be used to preserve trailing spaces that would otherwise be stripped. Text blocks are especially useful for embedding JSON, HTML, SQL, or any multi-line content directly in code without escaping every quote. They still produce a regular java.lang.String at compile time (interned like any literal) — Java does not have built-in string interpolation, but text blocks combine well with String.formatted() for templating.
questions.explanation: Text blocks are like switching from typing an address on a single cramped line with forced commas and abbreviations to writing it naturally across multiple lines the way you'd write it on an envelope — the compiler figures out the 'natural margin' for you instead of you manually escaping every line break and quote.
key_takeaways: Text blocks use triple double-quotes (""") and let you write multi-line strings without escaping every line break and quote
